<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cybercrime: The Umbrella Term

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybercrime (Formal KTU Definition):** Any illegal act in which a computer, networked device, or network is the **source, target, or instrument of the crime**. Under the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)** and the **Indian Penal Code (IPC)**, cybercrimes are categorized as cognizable, non-bailable offences that traverse state and international jurisdictional boundaries.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Highway" Metaphor

Imagine the **Internet as a giant public highway system**:
- **Your computer** = Your car (with all your valuables inside).
- **The Network** = The roads, flyovers, and tunnels.
- **A Cybercriminal** = A thief who can clone your car keys, tamper with traffic signals, or chase you down the highway.

Just as real-world crimes have variants (theft, fraud, stalking, breaking-and-entering), the digital world has its own variants — and **four of the most common ones are the focus of this module**: Hacking, Phishing, Identity Theft, and Cyberstalking.

---

## 1.2 The Four Major Categories of Cybercrime

### 🔓 1. Hacking (Unauthorized Access)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hacking (Formal Definition):** The act of identifying and exploiting **vulnerabilities** in a computer system, network, or application to gain **unauthorized access**, manipulate data, disrupt services, or steal confidential information. A *hacker* is the perpetrator; an *ethical hacker* (white-hat) is an authorized security professional.

**Sub-classes of Hackers:**

| Category | Hat Color | Intent |
|---|---|---|
| Black Hat | ⚫ Black | Malicious, criminal |
| White Hat | ⚪ White | Defensive, authorized penetration testing |
| Grey Hat | 🩶 Grey | Curious, sometimes unauthorized |
| Script Kiddie | 🟡 Yellow | Uses pre-built tools, low skill |
| Hacktivist | 🔴 Red | Politically/socially motivated |

**🎯 Real-World Analogy:** Hacking is like a burglar who studies the blueprint of a building, finds a weak window latch, climbs in, and either robs the place or just plants a hidden camera. The "blueprint" is the system's source code or network topology; the "weak latch" is a software vulnerability.

---

### 🎣 2. Phishing (Digital Deception)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phishing (Formal Definition):** A **social engineering** attack in which the attacker masquerades as a **legitimate, trusted entity** (bank, employer, government agency) via electronic communication — typically email, SMS (smishing), or voice call (vishing) — to trick the victim into revealing **sensitive data** such as usernames, passwords, OTPs, or credit card numbers.

**🎯 Real-World Analogy:** Think of phishing as a **con artist who shows up at your door wearing a fake postman uniform, holding a fake letter**, and politely asks: *"Sir, please confirm your address and sign here — I need your Aadhaar number to deliver your parcel."* You hand over the details willingly because the disguise is convincing.

**Common Variants of Phishing:**

| Variant | Vector | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Email Phishing** | Bulk email | Generic lure (e.g., "Your account is locked") |
| **Spear Phishing** | Targeted email | Personalized using OSINT |
| **Whaling** | Targeted email | Targets C-suite executives |
| **Smishing** | SMS text | Short, urgent messages with malicious links |
| **Vishing** | Voice call | Caller ID spoofing |
| **Clone Phishing** | Email | Legitimate email is cloned + malicious link swapped |

---

### 🪪 3. Identity Theft (Persona Hijacking)

> [!NOTE]
> **Identity Theft (Formal Definition):** The **deliberate acquisition and use** of another individual's personally identifying information (PII) — such as name, Aadhaar/PAN number, date of birth, biometric data, financial credentials — **without lawful authority**, typically for economic gain, fraud, impersonation, or to facilitate other criminal activities.

**The "Identity Theft Kill Chain" (forensically significant):**

1. **Reconnaissance** — Dumpster diving, data breaches, social-media scraping.
2. **Acquisition** — Stealing wallet, hacking databases, phishing.
3. **Cashing Out** — Opening bank accounts, taking loans, fake passports.
4. **Cover-Up** — Synthetic identities, alias creation.

**🎯 Real-World Analogy:** Identity theft is like someone **photocopying your driving licence, forging your signature, walking into a bank, taking a ₹10 lakh loan in your name, and disappearing**. You only find out when the recovery agent calls *you*.

---

### 👤 4. Cyberstalking (Digital Harassment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyberstalking (Formal Definition):** The **repeated, unsolicited, and intimidating use of electronic communications** to harass, threaten, monitor, or frighten a victim. It involves persistent unwanted contact via emails, social media, messaging apps, or tracking software, causing the victim to fear for their safety.

**Behavioral Indicators (Forensic Red Flags):**
- Fake social-media profiles impersonating or targeting the victim.
- Repeated unwanted messages after explicit "stop" requests.
- GPS tracking via spyware (Stalkerware).
- Publication of private information (**Doxxing**).
- Threats of physical harm.

**🎯 Real-World Analogy:** Cyberstalking is the **21st-century version of a stranger lurking outside your house every night** — but now they sit in another city, watching your Instagram stories, liking your 2017 photos, sending anonymous messages, and possibly tracking your phone's GPS. The **physical wall is gone, but the fear is real**.

---

## 1.3 Classification Matrix at a Glance

| Crime Type | Primary Motive | Primary Vector | Victim Awareness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hacking | Curiosity / Profit / Espionage | Network/Application vulnerabilities | Often delayed |
| Phishing | Credential harvesting | Email / SMS / Voice | Often delayed |
| Identity Theft | Financial fraud / Impersonation | Data breaches / Phishing / Physical | Often months/years later |
| Cyberstalking | Control / Revenge / Obsession | Social media / Messaging apps | Immediate but ongoing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hacking — Deep Analysis

### 🔬 The Operational Anatomy of a Hacking Attack

Every hacking incident, forensically, can be decomposed into **five logical phases** (the so-called "Cyber Kill Chain" — Lockheed Martin model, academically adopted):

1. **Reconnaissance (Passive + Active)** — The attacker gathers information. Passive: WHOIS lookup, Google dorking, Shodan searches. Active: Port scanning, ping sweeps.
2. **Weaponization** — Creating a deliverable payload (e.g., PDF with embedded exploit, malicious USB).
3. **Delivery** — Transmitting the weapon (email, web, USB, supply-chain injection).
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability (buffer overflow, SQL injection, zero-day).
5. **Installation & Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing persistent backdoor access via a C2 channel (e.g., HTTPS, DNS tunneling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Significance:** Every phase leaves **artifacts** — IP addresses in logs, modified registry keys, unusual outbound DNS queries, file-system timestamps. A forensic investigator reconstructs the attack by chaining these artifacts in **chronological order of volatility** (RFC 3227).

---

## 2.2 Phishing — Deep Analysis

### 🔬 The Anatomy of a Phishing Campaign

1. **Lure Crafting** — Attacker designs a convincing pretext (bank alert, job offer, COVID refund).
2. **Infrastructure Setup** — Domain registration (typosquatting: `paypa1.com`), compromised legitimate server, URL shorteners.
3. **Distribution** — Bulk mailing via botnets (e.g., Emotet, Necurs).
4. **Victim Interaction** — Click → credential-harvesting page (often on HTTPS with valid cert via Let's Encrypt).
5. **Data Exfiltration** — Stolen creds sent to attacker's drop server (often via Telegram bots or pastebin).

**Forensic Indicator — Email Header Analysis:**
- `Received:` chain hop-count anomalies.
- `Return-Path` ≠ `From:` (spoofing indicator).
- SPF, DKIM, DMARC alignment failures.
- Mismatched `Reply-To` domain.

---

## 2.3 Identity Theft — Deep Analysis

### 🔬 Categories of PII Targeted

| PII Category | Examples | Black-Market Value (Indicative) |
|---|---|---|
| Financial | Card number, CVV, bank login | ₹500 – ₹5,000 per record |
| Identity | Aadhaar, PAN, Passport | ₹1,000 – ₹10,000 per record |
| Medical | Insurance ID, diagnosis | ₹2,000 – ₹20,000 per record |
| Biometric | Fingerprint, iris (rare) | Extremely high, often unrecoverable |
| Synthetic | Fabricated combos | Used for loan fraud rings |

### 🔬 The Identity Theft Equation (Risk Quantification)

In cyber forensics and risk management, identity-theft loss to an organization is often quantified using the **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** model:

$$
SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor
$$

And the **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)**:

$$
ALE = SLE \times Annualized\_Rate\_of\_Occurrence
$$

These are **forensically and audit-defensively important** because they justify the cost of incident-response teams and insurance premiums.

---

## 2.4 Cyberstalking — Deep Analysis

### 🔬 The Stalking Escalation Ladder (Forensic Psychology Model)

| Level | Behavior | Legal Status (India — IT Act + IPC) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Online friendship, harmless comments | Not criminal |
| 2 | Repeated unwanted contact | Section 506 IPC (criminal intimidation) |
| 3 | Surveillance via social media, GPS | Section 66E IT Act (privacy violation) |
| 4 | Threats, doxxing, impersonation | Sections 66C, 66D IT Act + 507 IPC |
| 5 | Physical harm, extortion | Sections 384, 503 IPC + NDPS (if threats) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stalkerware (Consumer-grade spyware)** — Apps like FlexiSpy, mSpy, Cerberus — are a growing forensic concern. They run in **stealth mode**, survive factory resets, and exfiltrate GPS, SMS, call logs, and ambient audio. Detection requires **forensic imaging of the device's full storage** and behavioral analysis of network traffic.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Risk Quantification | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Unitless score (qualitative) |
| Single Loss Expectancy | $SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor$ | Currency (₹) |
| Annual Loss Expectancy | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Currency per year |
| Return on Security Investment | $ROSI = \frac{ALE - Control\_Cost}{Control\_Cost}$ | Ratio (decimal) |
| Phishing Click-Through Rate | $CTR_{phish} = \frac{Clicks}{Delivered} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Breach Detection Time | $MTTD = \frac{\sum Detection\_Time_i}{N_{incidents}}$ | Hours / Days |
| Containment Time | $MTTC = \frac{\sum Containment\_Time_i}{N_{incidents}}$ | Hours / Days |
| Mean Time to Recovery | $MTTR = MTTD + MTTC$ | End-to-end recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why These Matter in KTU:** In 14-mark analytical questions, examiners often expect you to **apply the SLE/ALE framework to a hypothetical cybercrime scenario** (e.g., "If a phishing breach exposes 10,000 customer records, and each record has an asset value of ₹500 with a 40% exposure factor, and the breach happens twice a year, calculate the ALE"). Master these formulas.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of These Concepts |
|---|---|
| Banking & FinTech | Real-time phishing detection using URL reputation + ML |
| Telecom | Stalkerware detection in CDR/IPDR analysis |
| E-Governance | Aadhaar-masked authentication to mitigate identity theft |
| Law Enforcement | Cybercrime cell evidence handling (chain of custody) |
| Insurance | Cyber-insurance premium calculation via ALE |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a Phishing Breach

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A regional bank's database stores **10,000 customer records**. Each record is valued at **₹800** (regulatory + reputational asset value). The **Exposure Factor (EF)** — the percentage of the asset value lost if a single record is compromised — is **40%**. Historical data shows such breaches occur on average **3 times per year**. **Calculate the SLE, ALE, and ROI of a proposed anti-phishing control costing ₹1,50,000 per year.**

---

### 🪜 Step 1: Identify the Given Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of records} &= N = 10{,}000 \\
\text{Asset Value per record} &= AV = ₹800 \\
\text{Exposure Factor} &= EF = 0.40 \\
\text{Annualized Rate of Occurrence} &= ARO = 3 \text{ incidents/year} \\
\text{Annual Cost of Proposed Control} &= C_{control} = ₹1{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We are isolating every input as a named variable. This is the **first forensic move** — document every assumption.

---

### 🪜 Step 2: Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SLE &= AV \times EF \\
    &= ₹800 \times 0.40 \\
    &= ₹320 \text{ per record}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** If a single customer's record is compromised, the bank loses 40% of its value — that is ₹320 per record. This is the *unit loss*.

---

### 🪜 Step 3: Compute the Total Loss per Single Breach Incident

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss}_{single} &= N \times SLE \\
                    &= 10{,}000 \times ₹320 \\
                    &= ₹32{,}00{,}000 \\
                    &= ₹32 \text{ Lakh per incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** A single breach affects all 10,000 records, so we **scale up** the unit loss by the population at risk.

---

### 🪜 Step 4: Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ALE &= Loss_{single} \times ARO \\
    &= ₹32{,}00{,}000 \times 3 \\
    &= ₹96{,}00{,}000 \\
    &= ₹96 \text{ Lakh per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** ALE = total *expected* loss per year. Even though each breach is ₹32 Lakh, expecting 3 such breaches per year raises the risk exposure to ₹96 Lakh.

---

### 🪜 Step 5: Compute the Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROSI &= \frac{ALE - C_{control}}{C_{control}} \\
     &= \frac{₹96{,}00{,}000 - ₹1{,}50{,}000}{₹1{,}50{,}000} \\
     &= \frac{₹94{,}50{,}000}{₹1{,}50{,}000} \\
     &= 63.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** A ROSI of **63.0** means **every ₹1 spent on the control yields ₹63 of risk reduction** — a *massive* positive ROI. This is the standard justification for security investments in boardrooms and audit reports.

---

### 🪜 Step 6: Forensic Decision Verdict

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Investing ₹1,50,000 in an anti-phishing control prevents an expected ₹96 Lakh in losses. **The control is overwhelmingly justified.** This is also the kind of cost-benefit analysis a forensic expert must present in court to demonstrate *reasonable security measures* under Section 43A of the IT Act.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Risk Profile of the Four Crimes

| Dimension | Hacking | Phishing | Identity Theft | Cyberstalking |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Motive** | Profit / Espionage | Credential theft | Financial fraud | Obsession / Control |
| **Attack Surface** | Servers, Apps, IoT | Email, SMS, Voice | Data stores, Humans | Personal devices, Social Media |
| **Skill Required** | High (technical) | Low–Medium | Low (uses tools) | Low (uses OSINT) |
| **Average Detection Time (MTTD)** | 200+ days (IBM 2023) | < 1 hour (user click) | 250+ days | Days to months |
| **Primary IT Act Sections** | 43, 66, 66F | 66C, 66D | 66C, 66D, 420 IPC | 66E, 67, 506 IPC |
| **Forensic Evidence Type** | Logs, malware binaries | Email headers, URLs | DB dumps, transaction logs | Chat logs, screenshots, GPS |
| **Chain-of-Custody Challenge** | Volatile memory, remote logs | Email server jurisdiction | Cross-border cloud storage | Mobile-device acquisition |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Cybercrimes (Module 1 Focus)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CYBERCRIMES] --> B[Against Persons]
    A --> C[Against Property]
    A --> D[Against Government]
    A --> E[Against Society]

    B --> B1[Cyberstalking]
    B --> B2[Identity Theft]
    B --> B3[Child Exploitation]
    B --> B4[Cyberbullying]

    C --> C1[Hacking]
    C --> C2[Phishing]
    C --> C3[Online Fraud]
    C --> C4[Data Theft]

    D --> D1[Cyber Terrorism]
    D --> D2[Cyber Warfare]

    E --> E1[Online Drug Trafficking]
    E --> E2[Spreading Obscene Material]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This **hierarchical taxonomy** classifies the four crimes in this module into the standard **legal-academic cybercrime taxonomy**. *Hacking* and *Phishing* fall under "Against Property"; *Identity Theft* and *Cyberstalking* fall under "Against Persons". This mapping is frequently tested in KTU Part A questions.

---

## 4.2 Cyber Kill Chain — Hacking Attack Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[RECONNAISSANCE] --> W[WEAPONIZATION]
    W --> D[DELIVERY]
    D --> E[EXPLOITATION]
    E --> I[INSTALLATION]
    I --> C2[COMMAND AND CONTROL]
    C2 --> A[ACTIONS ON OBJECTIVES]
    A -.-> FA[FORENSIC ARTIFACTS LEFT]
    FA --> LOGS[Network Logs]
    FA --> REG[Registry Changes]
    FA --> FS[File System Timestamps]
    FA --> MEM[Memory Residues]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each forward arrow is a **phase progression**; the dotted arrow at the end indicates that **every phase leaves forensic artifacts** — which is precisely what an investigator hunts for.

---

## 4.3 Phishing Attack Lifecycle — Social Engineering Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker as Attacker
    participant Infra as Phishing Infra
    participant Victim as Victim
    participant Drop as Drop Server

    Attacker->>Infra: Register typosquatted domain + clone login page
    Attacker->>Victim: Send spoofed email with urgent lure
    Victim->>Infra: Clicks link, lands on HTTPS clone
    Victim->>Infra: Submits username + password + OTP
    Infra->>Drop: POST credentials via Telegram bot
    Drop-->>Attacker: Credentials available
    Attacker->>Victim: Logs into real bank account
```

**Reading the diagram:** This **sequence diagram** traces a typical phishing incident end-to-end. Note the **delivery vector (email)**, the **exploitation of trust (HTTPS clone)**, and the **exfiltration channel (Telegram bot — a common 2024 trend)**.

---

## 4.4 Identity Theft — Acquisition-to-Cash-Out Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STAGE 1 RECONNAISSANCE] --> S2[STAGE 2 ACQUISITION]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 VALIDATION]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 EXPLOITATION]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5 COVER UP]

    S1 --> S1a[OSINT via social media]
    S1 --> S1b[Data breach dumps]

    S2 --> S2a[Phishing]
    S2 --> S2b[Physical theft]
    S2 --> S2c[Malware keyloggers]

    S3 --> S3a[Test with low-value txn]
    S3 --> S3b[Sell on dark web]

    S4 --> S4a[Open fraudulent accounts]
    S4 --> S4b[Apply for loans]
    S4 --> S4c[Tax refund fraud]

    S5 --> S5a[Synthetic ID creation]
    S5 --> S5b[Alias layered accounts]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A **multi-stage flow** showing how identity theft progresses. Forensic investigators must understand that **stage transitions** generate different evidence types — Stage 2 leaves phishing artifacts; Stage 4 leaves banking transaction logs.

---

## 4.5 Cyberstalking — Escalation & Forensics Mapping

```mermaid
graph TB
    L1[LEVEL 1 INITIAL OBSERVATION] --> L2[LEVEL 2 REPEATED CONTACT]
    L2 --> L3[LEVEL 3 DIGITAL SURVEILLANCE]
    L3 --> L4[LEVEL 4 THREATS AND DOXXING]
    L4 --> L5[LEVEL 5 PHYSICAL ESCALATION]

    L1 -.Evidence.-> E1[Browser history, follow list]
    L2 -.Evidence.-> E2[Screenshots, chat logs]
    L3 -.Evidence.-> E3[Stalkerware APKs, GPS logs]
    L4 -.Evidence.-> E4[Doxx archives, threat emails]
    L5 -.Evidence.-> E5[CCTV, mobile tower dumps]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **escalation ladder** maps each behavioral level to the **type of digital evidence** a forensic investigator must collect. Note the dotted "Evidence" arrows — they are the investigator's collection roadmap.

---

## 4.6 Functional Architecture — Cybercrime Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INC[INCIDENT REPORT] --> TRIAGE[TRIAGE AND CLASSIFICATION]
    TRIAGE --> IDENT[IDENTIFY CRIME TYPE]
    IDENT --> H[Hacking]
    IDENT --> P[Phishing]
    IDENT --> IT[Identity Theft]
    IDENT --> CS[Cyberstalking]

    H --> H1[Disk Imaging]
    H --> H2[Log Analysis]
    H --> H3[Malware Reverse Eng]

    P --> P1[Email Header Analysis]
    P --> P2[URL Sandbox Detonation]
    P --> P3[Credential Dump Search]

    IT --> IT1[DB Forensics]
    IT --> IT2[Financial Trail Tracing]
    IT --> IT3[Dark Web Monitoring]

    CS --> CS1[Mobile Forensics]
    CS --> CS2[Social Media OSINT]
    CS --> CS3[Stalkerware Detection]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **functional investigation pipeline**. Each crime type branches into its own **specialized forensic sub-workflow** — demonstrating that the type of cybercrime dictates the toolset, chain-of-custody protocol, and reporting standard.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between hacking and ethical hacking. Mention any two relevant provisions of the Indian IT Act, 2000.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarding Scheme):

- **Hacking (1 mark):** Hacking is the *unauthorized* intrusion into a computer system or network with malicious intent — such as data theft, service disruption, or system damage. It is punishable under **Section 66 of the IT Act, 2000** (computer-related offences) and Section 379/411 of the IPC (theft/cheating).

- **Ethical Hacking (1 mark):** Ethical hacking is the *authorized, legal* practice of probing a system's defenses — with the owner's written consent — to discover vulnerabilities *before* malicious hackers exploit them. Professionals holding certifications like **CEH (Certified Ethical Hacker)** or **OSCP** perform it.

- **IT Act Provisions (1 mark):**
  - **Section 43** — Penalty for damage to computer systems (civil remedy).
  - **Section 66** — Computer-related offences (criminal remedy, up to 3 years imprisonment or ₹5 Lakh fine).

---

### ❓ Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**List any four types of phishing attacks and explain how identity theft is enabled by a successful phishing campaign.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarding Scheme):

**Four types of phishing (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Email Phishing** — Mass-mailed fraudulent messages.
2. **Spear Phishing** — Targeted emails personalized using OSINT.
3. **Smishing** — Phishing via SMS.
4. **Vishing** — Phishing via voice/telephone calls.

**How phishing enables identity theft (1 mark):** A successful phishing attack harvests **personally identifiable information (PII)** — such as Aadhaar, PAN, date of birth, or bank credentials — which the attacker then reuses or sells to commit **identity theft** (opening fraudulent accounts, taking loans, or filing fake tax returns in the victim's name). This is the **bridge vector** that converts a single phishing email into a multi-crore identity-fraud operation.

---

## 📕 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by **(a) writing generic answers without naming specific IT Act sections**, **(b) ignoring the forensic-investigation angle**, and **(c) failing to give real-world examples**. Always **name sections, name tools, and cite one real incident**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the different categories of computer hackers. Explain the **Cyber Kill Chain** model with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Analyze a real-world hacking incident (e.g., the **WannaCry ransomware attack of 2017**) under the lens of forensic investigation. What artifacts would you look for? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Hacker Categories + Cyber Kill Chain (7 Marks)**

**Hacker Categories (4 Marks — 1 each for any four):**

| # | Category | Description | Intent |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Black Hat** | Classic criminal hacker | Malicious — steals, defaces, extorts |
| 2 | **White Hat** | Security professional, works defensively | Authorized penetration testing |
| 3 | **Grey Hat** | Neither fully legal nor criminal | Probes without permission, often publicizes |
| 4 | **Script Kiddie** | Low-skill individual using ready-made tools | Mostly nuisance, sometimes harmful |
| 5 | **Hacktivist** | Politically/ideologically motivated | Anonymous is the most famous example |
| 6 | **Insider Threat** | Employee with malicious intent | Has legitimate access — hardest to detect |

**Cyber Kill Chain — Lockheed Martin (3 Marks):**

The Cyber Kill Chain, developed by Lockheed Martin in 2011, is a **7-stage forensic framework** that breaks a hacking attack into traceable phases:

1. **Reconnaissance** — Passive (OSINT) and active (port scanning).
2. **Weaponization** — Coupling exploit with a deliverable (e.g., PDF + macro).
3. **Delivery** — Email, USB, web drive-by, supply-chain.
4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability.
5. **Installation** — Backdoor, rootkit, persistence mechanism.
6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Beaconing to attacker's server.
7. **Actions on Objectives** — Data exfiltration, ransomware encryption, destruction.

**Forensic Significance:** Investigators correlate logs, memory dumps, and endpoint telemetry with each stage to **reconstruct the attacker's path**.

---

**(b) Real-World Analysis — WannaCry 2017 (7 Marks)**

**Background (1.5 Marks):**
On 12 May 2017, the **WannaCry ransomware** cryptographically locked over **2,30,000 computers in 150 countries** within 24 hours. It exploited the **EternalBlue SMBv1 vulnerability** (MS17-010) originally developed by the NSA and leaked by the Shadow Brokers group. Victims included the UK's NHS, Renault factories, and India's Andhra Pradesh Police.

**Forensic Phases (5.5 Marks — 0.5 each artifact):**

| Investigation Phase | Forensic Action | Artifact / Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| **Initial Detection** | SIEM alert on mass SMB traffic spike | Network flow logs |
| **Volatile Capture** | Live memory dump of infected host | `.raw` / `.lime` memory image; in-memory encryption keys |
| **Disk Imaging** | Bit-stream image of C: drive | `.E01` / `.dd` image |
| **Malware Analysis** | Static (hash, strings) + Dynamic (sandbox) | WannaCry binary, kill-switch domain `.com` |
| **Network Forensics** | PCAP analysis of SMB propagation | Wireshark capture, pcap of SMB exploit packets |
| **Log Analysis** | Windows Event Logs + Sysmon | Event ID 4624, 4720 (user account creation), 7045 (service install) |
| **Registry Analysis** | Check Run/RunOnce keys | Persistence mechanism entries |
| **Timeline Reconstruction** | Plaso / log2timeline | Master timeline mapping kill-chain stages |
| **Indicator Extraction** | Hashes, IPs, domains, YARA rules | IOC list shared with CERT-In |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Examiner's Note):** Students often describe WannaCry *only* as a "virus attack" without explaining the **vulnerability lifecycle** (leaked NSA exploit → Shadow Brokers → EternalBlue → WannaCry integration). Always show the **chain of custody of the exploit itself** in your answer to score full marks.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** What is phishing? Explain with a diagram the **anatomy of a phishing email attack**. Describe the role of **email header analysis** in forensic investigation of phishing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **identity theft** in detail. Explain its lifecycle, the categories of PII targeted, and the relevant legal provisions under Indian law. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Phishing + Email Header Forensics (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Phishing is a **social-engineering attack** in which the attacker impersonates a trusted entity (bank, employer, government) via digital communication to trick victims into disclosing sensitive data such as login credentials, OTPs, or financial information.

**Anatomy of a Phishing Email (3 Marks — with diagram reference):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FROM SPOOFED ADDRESS] --> B[URGENT SUBJECT LINE]
    B --> C[GENERIC OR PERSONALIZED GREETING]
    C --> D[EMBEDDED MALICIOUS LINK]
    D --> E[FAKE LOGIN PAGE HTTPS]
    E --> F[DATA SUBMITTED TO ATTACKER]
    F --> G[CREDENTIALS STOLEN]
```

**Component-by-Component (0.5 each):**
1. **Spoofed Sender** — `support@paypa1.com` (typosquatting).
2. **Urgency Cue** — *"Your account will be suspended in 24 hours"*.
3. **Personalization** — Uses victim's name (from breached DBs).
4. **Malicious Link** — Hover text differs from actual URL.
5. **Clone Page** — Pixel-perfect copy hosted on HTTPS.
6. **Data Capture** — Form submits to attacker's drop server.

**Email Header Forensics (3 Marks):**
The forensic investigator examines the **RFC 5322 headers** in this order:
- **`Received:` chain** — Traces the email's path across mail servers.
- **`Return-Path:`** — Where bounces go (often differs from `From:`).
- **`Authentication-Results:`** — SPF, DKIM, DMARC pass/fail status.
- **`Reply-To:`** — Often points to attacker-controlled address.
- **Hop-count anomalies** — Inconsistencies between claimed origin and actual hops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always mention **SPF, DKIM, and DMARC** by name. A 14-mark answer that omits these protocols is considered incomplete in KTU's 2024 valuation rubric.

---

**(b) Identity Theft — Lifecycle + PII + Law (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Identity theft is the *unauthorized acquisition and fraudulent use* of another person's **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)** for economic gain or impersonation.

**Lifecycle (3 Marks):**

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| 1. **Reconnaissance** | OSINT via social media; breached-data dumps on dark web |
| 2. **Acquisition** | Phishing, dumpster diving, insider theft, malware |
| 3. **Validation** | Low-value test transactions to validate stolen data |
| 4. **Cashing Out** | Fraudulent loans, tax refund claims, account takeover |
| 5. **Cover-Up** | Synthetic identity creation, alias layering |

**PII Categories (1.5 Marks):**
- **Government-issued IDs** — Aadhaar, PAN, Passport, Driving Licence.
- **Financial** — Card numbers, CVV, bank account credentials.
- **Biometric** — Fingerprint, iris, facial geometry (emerging concern).
- **Demographic** — DOB, address, phone, email.
- **Behavioral** — Keystroke patterns, location history.

**Indian Legal Provisions (1.5 Marks):**
- **IT Act Section 66C** — Identity theft (punishable with up to **3 years imprisonment** and ₹1 Lakh fine).
- **IT Act Section 66D** — Cheating by personation using computer resource.
- **IPC Section 420** — Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property.
- **Aadhaar Act Section 29** — Penalty for unauthorized access to Aadhaar data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Many students cite **only Section 66C** and miss Section 66D. Always cross-reference **both IT Act + IPC** provisions — KTU examiners reward the ability to **integrate multiple statutes**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before your exam):**

- ✅ The four cybercrimes in this module are **Hacking, Phishing, Identity Theft, and Cyberstalking** — they fall under two legal buckets: *Against Property* (Hacking, Phishing) and *Against Persons* (Identity Theft, Cyberstalking).
- ✅ Hacking follows the **Cyber Kill Chain** — Reconnaissance → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives.
- ✅ Phishing has six variants — **Email, Spear, Whaling, Smishing, Vishing, Clone** — know at least four by name.
- ✅ Email header forensics relies on **Received chain, Return-Path, Reply-To, SPF/DKIM/DMARC** — these are *examiner favourites*.
- ✅ Identity theft uses **PII (Aadhaar, PAN, biometrics, financial data)** — punishable under **Section 66C and 66D IT Act** + **Section 420 IPC**.
- ✅ Cyberstalking is an **escalation crime** — be ready to map behavior levels to **legal sections** (66E, 506 IPC, 507 IPC).
- ✅ Master the **ALE / SLE / ROSI formulas** — they appear in 7-mark sub-questions regularly.
- ✅ For **14-mark answers**, always combine **definition + diagram + lifecycle + IT Act section + real-world example** — this is the *gold-standard* KTU answer structure.
- ✅ Remember the **Stalkerware detection** angle in cyberstalking forensics — examiners reward modern, tool-aware answers.
- ✅ End every answer with a **forensic-investigation angle** — this is what differentiates a generic answer from a **PEITT755-grade answer**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Evidence: Collection, Preservation, and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Evidence: Collection, Preservation, and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Evidence** is defined as any probative information stored or transmitted in binary form that a court of law or competent authority may accept as reliable proof of an event, action, or identity within a computing system or network infrastructure.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT755 – Cyber and Network Forensics)**, digital evidence encompasses **any electronic artifact** — files, logs, metadata, network packets, volatile memory dumps, registry entries, emails, and deleted data fragments — that can be used to reconstruct a security incident, an intrusion, or a criminal act.

The three governing pillars of digital evidence handling are:

| Pillar | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Collection** | The systematic, forensically-sound acquisition of digital artifacts from electronic devices. |
| **Preservation** | The process of maintaining the **integrity**, **authenticity**, and **chain of custody** of the acquired data. |
| **Analysis** | The scientific examination, correlation, and interpretation of digital artifacts to derive actionable intelligence. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Digital Fingerprint"**

Imagine a traditional crime scene where a detective carefully lifts **fingerprints** from a glass surface using specialized powder and tape. The detective does not smudge the print, photographs it before lifting, and seals it in an evidence bag with a documented **chain of custody**.

Digital evidence works **identically**:

- The **glass** is a hard drive, SSD, smartphone, or server.
- The **fingerprint** is a deleted file, log entry, registry hive, or network packet.
- The **specialized powder** is a write-blocker (hardware/software).
- The **evidence bag** is a forensic image file (e.g., `.E01`, `.DD`).
- The **chain of custody** is the documented logbook tracking who handled the evidence and when.

Just as a detective would never touch a fingerprint with bare hands (to avoid contamination), a forensic investigator **never works on the original evidence**. They always create a **bit-stream copy** and verify its integrity using cryptographic **hash functions**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally applied across all KTU forensic laboratory calculations:

- **Standard Hash Algorithms**: MD5 (128-bit), SHA-1 (160-bit), SHA-256 (256-bit).
- **Sector Size (Legacy HDDs)**: **512 bytes** per sector.
- **Advanced Format (AF) Drives**: **4096 bytes** (4 KB) per sector.
- **Memory Page Size (x86/x64)**: **4096 bytes**.
- **Minimum Forensic Image Verification**: Two independent hash algorithms (e.g., MD5 + SHA-1).
- **Slack Space per Cluster**: $C_{slack} = C_{cluster} - L_{file}$ bytes.
- **Volatility Timer (RFC 3227)**: Registers should be captured within **seconds**; RAM within **seconds-to-minutes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Digital Evidence Lifecycle

The digital evidence lifecycle follows the **Scientific Working Group on Digital Evidence (SWGDE)** framework, adapted by KTU for the 2024 syllabus:

1. **Identification** — Recognizing potential digital evidence sources (computers, mobile devices, IoT, cloud).
2. **Preservation** — Securing the scene; applying physical and logical safeguards.
3. **Collection** — Acquiring data using forensically-sound tools and verified procedures.
4. **Examination / Analysis** — Reducing and processing the data to extract relevant information.
5. **Presentation** — Reporting findings in a legally admissible, reproducible format.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters**: KTU examiners specifically look for whether a student understands that **analysis without preservation** renders evidence inadmissible due to contamination. Marks are deducted when students skip the preservation step.

## 2.2 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)

The **Order of Volatility** dictates which data sources must be captured first because they decay or are overwritten rapidly:

| Priority | Data Source | Approximate Lifespan |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 (Highest)** | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds |
| **2** | Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table | Seconds |
| **3** | Kernel Statistics, Memory (RAM) | Minutes |
| **4** | Temporary File Systems | Hours |
| **5** | Disk Files / File System | Days–Years |
| **6 (Lowest)** | Remote Logs, Backups | Years |

## 2.3 Chain of Custody

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chain of Custody** is the **unbroken, documented, and time-stamped trail** that records the seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of digital evidence. Any break in this chain can result in the evidence being ruled **inadmissible** in a court of law.

### Mandatory Documentation Fields:

- **Case Number & Unique Evidence ID**
- **Investigator Name, Badge/ID, Contact**
- **Date and Time of Seizure** (with timezone)
- **Location of Seizure** (GPS coordinates for mobile)
- **Hash Values** (MD5 + SHA-1 or SHA-256)
- **Storage Medium Serial Number**
- **Reason for Seizure / Affidavit Reference**
- **Signature of Receiving Officer** (for every transfer)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Critical Formulas for Digital Evidence Calculations**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MD5 Hash Output** | $H_{MD5} = 128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | Hexadecimal: **32 characters** | First-line integrity check |
| **SHA-256 Hash Output** | $H_{SHA256} = 256 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Hexadecimal: **64 characters** | Cryptographic-grade integrity |
| **Hash Match Verification** | $H_{orig} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{copy}$ | Boolean: $\text{True}$ $\vert$ $\text{False}$ | Admissibility proof |
| **Slack Space** | $S_{slack} = C_{cluster} - L_{file}$ | Bytes ($C$: cluster size, $L$: file logical size) | Recovering deleted data |
| **Drive Space (Sectors)** | $T_{bytes} = N_{sectors} \times 512$ | Bytes ($N$: number of sectors) | Image size estimation |
| **Drive Space (AF)** | $T_{bytes} = N_{sectors} \times 4096$ | Bytes ($N$: number of sectors) | Modern SSD/AF drive sizing |
| **Bad-Sector Hash Skip** | $H_{final} = H_{computed} \oplus H_{badsector}$ | XOR operation | Forensic image verification |
| **File Carving Success Rate** | $P_{carve} = \dfrac{F_{recovered}}{F_{original}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) | Tool effectiveness metric |
| **Disk Imaging Time** | $t_{image} = \dfrac{S_{disk}}{R_{interface}}$ | Seconds ($S$: disk size in bytes, $R$: interface speed in B/s) | Acquisition planning |
| **Email Header Authenticity** | $V_{DKIM} = \text{SHA256}(\text{header} \vert \vert \text{body})$ | Boolean match against DNS key | Phishing investigation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs)** apply this lifecycle within minutes of detecting a breach.
- **Law Enforcement (CBI, Interpol, FBI)** relies on these standards to ensure evidence withstands cross-border litigation.
- **Corporate Counsel** uses preserved evidence in civil suits involving data theft, IP infringement, and insider threats.
- **KTU graduates** entering the cybersecurity workforce must demonstrate mastery of these protocols to pass industry certifications such as **GCFA**, **EnCE**, and **CCE**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hash-Based Evidence Integrity Verification

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to demonstrate the mathematical basis of forensic image verification. Below is the **complete step-by-step derivation**.

### Problem Context:
A forensic investigator captures a forensic image of a 1 TB hard drive. The investigator computes the MD5 hash of both the original drive and the image file. Both hashes must match exactly for the evidence to be considered **forensically sound**.

### Step 1 — Define the Hash Function

Let $D$ denote the **original digital evidence** (binary data stream of length $n$ bits).

$$D = \{b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots, b_n\}$$

where each $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ represents a binary digit.

### Step 2 — Apply the MD5 Algorithm

The MD5 algorithm processes $D$ in **512-bit (64-byte) blocks**. Let $B_k$ represent the $k$-th block:

$$B_k = \{b_{(k-1) \cdot 512 + 1}, \ldots, b_{k \cdot 512}\}$$

The MD5 algorithm initializes four 32-bit registers (A, B, C, D) and processes each block through **64 rounds** of non-linear functions:

$$F(B, C, D) = (B \land C) \lor (\neg B \land D)$$
$$G(B, C, D) = (B \land D) \lor (C \land \neg D)$$
$$H(B, C, D) = B \oplus C \oplus D$$
$$I(B, C, D) = C \oplus (B \lor \neg D)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Final Digest

After processing all blocks, the four 32-bit registers are concatenated:

$$H_{MD5}(D) = A_{final} \Vert B_{final} \Vert C_{final} \Vert D_{final}$$

This produces a fixed-length output of **128 bits**, regardless of input size.

### Step 4 — Verification of the Forensic Image

Let $I$ be the **forensic image** created from $D$. Compute:

$$H_{orig} = \text{MD5}(D)$$
$$H_{image} = \text{MD5}(I)$$

The evidence is **admissible if and only if**:

$$H_{orig} = H_{image}$$

A single bit difference causes a **cascade (avalanche) effect**, producing a completely different hash. This is the **avalanche property**:

$$\Delta H = H_{orig} \oplus H_{image} \neq 0 \implies \text{Evidence Compromised}$$

### Step 5 — Multi-Algorithm Verification (Best Practice)

To meet **Daubert Standard** admissibility, KTU examiners recommend dual-hashing:

$$V_{final} = \begin{cases} \text{Admissible} & \text{if } H_{MD5}(D) = H_{MD5}(I) \text{ AND } H_{SHA256}(D) = H_{SHA256}(I) \\ \text{Inadmissible} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Slack Space Calculation

### Problem Context:
A file system uses a **cluster size** of 4096 bytes (4 KB). A user saves a file with a **logical size** of 1500 bytes. Compute the slack space available for hidden data recovery.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Variables

$$C_{cluster} = 4096 \text{ bytes}$$
$$L_{file} = 1500 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Slack Space Formula

$$S_{slack} = C_{cluster} - L_{file}$$

$$S_{slack} = 4096 - 1500$$

$$S_{slack} = 2596 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

The **2596 bytes** of slack space exist between the end of the file's logical data and the end of the allocated cluster. This space:

- Contains **old data** from previously deleted files (often recoverable).
- May contain **RAM-resident data** (e.g., fragments of memory pages).
- Is a **classic hiding spot** for steganographic payloads and malware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Always state that slack space is **NOT zeroed** by the operating system during file write operations on legacy file systems like FAT32 and NTFS (without TRIM).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Data Acquisition Methods

| Acquisition Type | Description | Pros | Cons | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Disk-to-Image (`.DD`, `.E01`)** | Bit-stream copy to a forensic image file | Most forensically sound; compressed; hashable | Requires storage ≥ original drive size | Standard criminal investigation |
| **Disk-to-Disk (Clone)** | Bit-stream copy to an identical-capacity drive | Bootable; useful for legacy systems | No compression; requires exact hardware match | Server seizure scenarios |
| **Logical Acquisition** | Copies only active file system files | Fast; minimal storage | Misses deleted files, slack, unallocated | Corporate email collection |
| **Sparse Acquisition** | Copies only allocated clusters | Reduces image size significantly | May miss hidden partitions | Quick triage in incident response |
| **Live / Volatile Acquisition** | Captures RAM, registry, network state | Captures encryption keys in memory | Modifies running system (must document) | Ransomware incident response |
| **Cross-Border Cloud Acquisition** | Subpoena-based retrieval from cloud providers | Accesses remote evidence | Jurisdictional complexity; volatile | Multi-national breach cases |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define **digital evidence** and list **any four** sources from which digital evidence can be collected in a cybercrime investigation.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Digital evidence is any information of probative value that is stored or transmitted in electronic form and may be relied upon in a court of law.

Four sources are:

1. **Computer systems** — desktops, laptops, servers (file systems, logs, registry).
2. **Mobile devices** — smartphones, tablets (call logs, SMS, GPS, app data).
3. **Network devices** — routers, firewalls, IDS/IPS logs.
4. **Cloud storage and IoT devices** — Dropbox, Google Drive, smart home devices.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** Explain the significance of the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** in digital evidence collection.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Order of Volatility is a prioritized list that dictates which data must be captured first based on how rapidly it decays. Registers and CPU cache must be captured within **seconds**, RAM within **minutes**, while disk-resident data persists for **years**. Failing to follow this order can cause **irreversible loss** of critical evidence such as running process information, encryption keys, and network connections.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question Paper, PEITT755]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Describe in detail the **five phases of the digital evidence lifecycle** as defined by the **Scientific Working Group on Digital Evidence (SWGDE)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Chain of Custody**. Discuss the **mandatory fields** that must be documented and the **legal implications** of breaking the chain. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Five Phases of the Digital Evidence Lifecycle:**

1. **Identification** — The investigator identifies all potential sources of digital evidence: computers, mobile devices, IoT, cloud accounts, removable media, and network logs. A thorough search warrant must specify the scope.
2. **Preservation** — The scene is secured to prevent tampering. Devices are photographed in situ, disconnected from networks, and placed in Faraday bags (mobile devices).
3. **Collection** — Volatile data is captured first (RAM, routing tables, process lists). Non-volatile data is acquired using **write-blockers** and forensic imaging tools (EnCase, FTK, `dd`).
4. **Examination / Analysis** — The forensic image is processed using tools like **Autopsy, X-Ways, Volatility, and Wireshark**. Deleted files are carved, timelines are reconstructed, and indicators of compromise (IOCs) are extracted.
5. **Presentation** — Findings are compiled into a **forensic report** that is reproducible, peer-reviewable, and admissible under the **Daubert Standard**. Reports must include tool versions, hash values, and methodology.

**(b) Chain of Custody — Mandatory Fields & Legal Implications:**

The Chain of Custody is the **unbroken, time-stamped, and signed** documentation trail tracking every person who handled the evidence from seizure to court presentation.

**Mandatory fields include:**

- Case number, evidence ID, description
- Investigator name, badge ID, and signature
- Date, time, and location of seizure
- Hash values (MD5 + SHA-1 or SHA-256) of the original and the image
- Storage medium serial number
- Transfer date, time, and recipient signature
- Final disposition (return, destruction, archival)

**Legal Implications:**

- A **break in the chain** can render evidence **inadmissible** under the **Daubert Standard** and Indian Evidence Act (Sections 45, 65B).
- Defense attorneys routinely challenge evidence by demonstrating undocumented transfers.
- Investigators may face **perjury charges** if chain-of-custody records are falsified.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the role of **cryptographic hash functions** in preserving the integrity of digital evidence. Compute the **MD5 and SHA-256 hashes** conceptually for a 10 MB forensic image and describe the **avalanche effect**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A forensic investigator seizes a hard drive with **2,000,000 sectors** of 512 bytes each. Calculate: **(i)** Total drive capacity, **(ii)** Imaging time if the SATA-3 interface transfers at 6 Gbps, **(iii)** Slack space for a 2200-byte file on a 4096-byte cluster. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Cryptographic Hash Functions and Evidence Integrity:**

Hash functions such as **MD5 (128-bit)** and **SHA-256 (256-bit)** map arbitrary-length input data to a fixed-length output called a **message digest** or **fingerprint**.

**Properties required for forensic use:**

1. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output.
2. **Avalanche Effect** — A 1-bit change in input changes ~50% of output bits.
3. **Collision Resistance** — Computationally infeasible to find two inputs with the same hash.
4. **Pre-image Resistance** — Cannot reverse-engineer the input from the hash.

**Procedure:**

$$H_{orig} = \text{MD5}(\text{Original Drive})$$
$$H_{image} = \text{MD5}(\text{Forensic Image})$$
$$H_{orig} = H_{image} \implies \text{Evidence Admissible}$$

**Avalanche Effect Example:**

$$H(\text{"CASE\_OPEN"}) = \texttt{a1b2c3d4...}$$
$$H(\text{"CASE\_OPEN!"}) = \texttt{9f8e7d6c...}$$

Changing one character (adding `!`) causes a complete hash change.

**(b) Numerical Calculations:**

**(i) Total Drive Capacity:**

$$T_{bytes} = N_{sectors} \times 512$$

$$T_{bytes} = 2{,}000{,}000 \times 512 = 1{,}024{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to gigabytes (decimal):

$$T_{GB} = \dfrac{1{,}024{,}000{,}000}{10^9} \approx 1.024 \text{ GB}$$

**(ii) Imaging Time (SATA-3 at 6 Gbps):**

SATA-3 uses 8b/10b encoding, so effective throughput is:

$$R_{effective} = \dfrac{6 \text{ Gbps}}{10} \times 8 = 4.8 \text{ Gbps} = 600 \text{ MB/s}$$

$$t_{image} = \dfrac{T_{bytes}}{R_{effective}}$$

$$t_{image} = \dfrac{1{,}024{,}000{,}000}{600 \times 10^6}$$

$$t_{image} \approx 1.707 \text{ seconds}$$

**(iii) Slack Space:**

$$S_{slack} = C_{cluster} - L_{file} = 4096 - 2200 = 1896 \text{ bytes}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Skipping Preservation Step**: Students often jump directly from "Incident Detection" to "Collection". Always include **scene securing** and **write-blocker application** as part of preservation.
> 2. **Confusing MD5 with Encryption**: MD5 is a **one-way hash**, NOT encryption. It cannot be decrypted. Writing "MD5 encryption" loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Ignoring Volatility Order**: Failing to mention that **RAM must be captured before disk** costs **3 marks** in long-answer questions.
> 4. **Single Hash Verification**: KTU 2024 expects **dual-hashing (MD5 + SHA-256)** for admissibility. Mentioning only MD5 is considered incomplete.
> 5. **No Chain of Custody Reference**: Any forensic process answer that omits **chain of custody** will be marked **incomplete** regardless of technical accuracy.
> 6. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Always show explicit conversion steps (e.g., bytes → KB → MB → GB). Skipping intermediate steps loses **partial marks**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Digital Evidence** = any probative electronic data admissible in court.
- ✅ **Three Pillars**: Collection, Preservation, Analysis.
- ✅ **Five Lifecycle Phases**: Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Presentation.
- ✅ **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**: Registers > Cache > Routing Table > RAM > Temp FS > Disk > Logs.
- ✅ **Hash Algorithms**: MD5 (128-bit), SHA-1 (160-bit), SHA-256 (256-bit).
- ✅ **Best Practice**: Use **dual-hashing** (MD5 + SHA-256) for admissibility.
- ✅ **Chain of Custody** is **mandatory** — any break = inadmissibility.
- ✅ **Write Blockers** (hardware preferred) must be used during acquisition.
- ✅ **Forensic Image Formats**: `.DD` (raw), `.E01` (EnCase compressed), `.AFF` (Advanced Forensic Format).
- ✅ **Slack Space Formula**: $S_{slack} = C_{cluster} - L_{file}$.
- ✅ **Sector Sizes**: 512 bytes (legacy) or 4096 bytes (Advanced Format).
- ✅ **Tools to Remember**: EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways, Volatility, Wireshark, `dd`, `dcfldd`.
- ✅ **Legal Frameworks**: Daubert Standard, Indian Evidence Act §65B (electronic evidence).
- ✅ **Avalanche Effect**: 1-bit change in input → ~50% change in hash output.
- ✅ **Volatile Acquisition Priority**: Always capture **RAM first** — it may contain encryption keys.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyber Forensics Tools: EnCase, Autopsy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cyber Forensics Tools: EnCase, Autopsy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cyber Forensics Tools — Formal Definition

**Cyber Forensics Tools** are specialized software applications designed to identify, preserve, analyze, and present digital evidence in a forensically sound manner so that the evidence remains admissible in a court of law. They operate on the principle of maintaining the **integrity**, **authenticity**, and **chain-of-custody** of digital artifacts (files, logs, memory dumps, network captures).

Within this category, two tools dominate the academic and professional landscape:

- **EnCase Forensic** (commercially licensed, developed by **Guidance Software**, now acquired by **OpenText**) — the de-facto industry standard used by law enforcement agencies and Fortune 500 corporate investigation units.
- **Autopsy** (open-source, distributed by **Basis Technology**, built atop **The Sleuth Kit / TSK**) — the most widely adopted free digital forensics platform, preferred in academic labs and small-to-medium scale investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The syllabus explicitly lists EnCase and Autopsy as representative tools to demonstrate *evidence acquisition*, *hash verification*, *file system analysis*, and *report generation* workflows. Mastery of their feature matrices is a frequent short-answer target in **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Detective's Toolbox

Imagine a **crime scene investigator (CSI)** arriving at a burglary. They do not just "look around"; they bring a *standardized toolkit*: fingerprint powder, evidence bags, a measuring tape, and a camera. Each tool has a precise role, and crucially, the investigator follows a fixed protocol so that defense lawyers cannot later argue the evidence was tampered with.

**EnCase** is like the *premium forensic laboratory kit* — a high-end, validated, court-defensible toolkit used by FBI-grade investigators. It produces **E01 evidence files** (proprietary but widely accepted) and comes with rigorous chain-of-custody reporting.

**Autopsy** is like the *open-source equivalent of the same lab kit*, built on the same scientific principles but freely available and extensible via plug-in modules. It reads the same evidence formats (raw/dd, E01 via **libewf**) and produces legally valid reports when operated correctly.

In both cases, the underlying science is the same: **digital evidence must be acquired without modification, hashed for integrity, and analyzed through reproducible methodology.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Forensic Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - **MD5 hash length:** **128 bits (32 hex characters)**
> - **SHA-1 hash length:** **160 bits (40 hex characters)**
> - **SHA-256 hash length:** **256 bits (64 hex characters)**
> - **E01 segment size:** **Default 640 MB per segment**
> - **Minimum recommended evidence storage:** **2× original disk size** (one for image, one for working copy)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 EnCase — Deep Dive

**EnCase Forensic** is a Windows-based integrated suite comprising four major functional components:

#### 2.1.1 Core EnCase Components

| Component | Function | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **EnCase Examiner** | The investigator's GUI; case management, bookmarking, keyword search | High — usually asked in Part A |
| **EnCase Forensic (Acquisition Module)** | Bit-stream imaging of source drives to E01/Lx01 format | Very High — acquisition workflow is a 7-mark favorite |
| **EnCase Evidence Processor** | Automated evidence extraction (registry, internet history, email) | Medium |
| **EnCase Reporting Module** | Generates legally formatted, court-ready reports | Medium |

#### 2.1.2 The EnCase Evidence File Format (E01 / Ex01)

The **E01 format** (also called **Expert Witness Format / EWFB**) wraps the raw bit-stream with forensic metadata:

- **Case information** (case number, examiner name, acquisition date)
- **Hash values** (MD5, optionally SHA-1) of each segment
- **CRC checksum** for error detection
- **Compression** (none / good / best)
- **Segmented structure** (default 640 MB per segment file)

The format is *self-authenticating*: any modification of a segment invalidates the stored hash and is immediately detectable.

#### 2.1.3 EnCase Acquisition Workflow — Step Logic

1. **Connect source media** to a write-blocker (hardware preferred over software).
2. **Open a new case** → assign case number, examiner ID, evidence tag.
3. **Acquire bit-stream image** to the destination storage in E01 format.
4. **Compute and store hash** (MD5 and SHA-1) at acquisition time.
5. **Verify acquired image** by re-computing the hash and comparing.
6. **Generate acquisition log** documenting chain-of-custody.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a write-blocker?** A write-blocker prevents any accidental write command from reaching the source drive, preserving its evidentiary state. This is the *single most critical* hardware component in any forensic acquisition.

### 2.2 Autopsy — Deep Dive

**Autopsy** is a GUI front-end to **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**, a collection of UNIX-based command-line forensic tools (`fls`, `icat`, `mmls`, `fsstat`, etc.). It is platform-independent (Windows/Linux/macOS) and **100% free / open-source**.

#### 2.2.1 Autopsy Architectural Layers

- **Top Layer (GUI):** HTML/JavaScript-based browser interface.
- **Middle Layer (Java Backend):** Core processing engine, module dispatcher.
- **Bottom Layer (The Sleuth Kit):** Low-level file system, volume, and disk analysis tools written in C/C++.

#### 2.2.2 Autopsy Ingest Modules (Selected)

| Module | Purpose | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Recent Activity** | Extracts web history, downloads, USB connections | Timeline + table |
| **Hash Lookup** | Compares file hashes against **NSRL** known-good database | Tagged files |
| **File Type Identification** | Uses **libmagic / Tika** instead of file extensions | Corrected MIME types |
| **Keyword Search** | Indexed and regular-expression search across files | Search hits |
| **Exif Parser** | Extracts GPS, camera, timestamp metadata from images | Metadata table |
| **Email Parser** | Parses MBOX, PST/OST (via `libpff`) | Email artifacts |

#### 2.2.3 Autopsy Supported Evidence Formats

- **Raw / dd images** (`.dd`, `.img`)
- **EWF / E01 images** (via **libewf** integration)
- **AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)** open standard
- **Logical files / folders** (local disks, USB drives)
- **Memory dumps** (via Volatility integration)

### 2.3 Integrity Verification — The Forensic Hash Equation

The fundamental invariant of any forensic acquisition is:

$$
H_{\text{original}}(M) \;\equiv\; H_{\text{acquired}}(M)
$$

Where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function applied to media $M$. If equality fails, the evidence has been *corrupted or tampered with*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hash equality test | $H_{\text{src}} = H_{\text{dst}}$ | $H$: hash output (hex bits) | Holds iff no bit changed |
| MD5 digest size | $L = 128$ bits | bits | Fixed |
| SHA-256 digest size | $L = 256$ bits | bits | Fixed |
| E01 segment split | $S_i = 640$ MB default | megabytes | Configurable 1 MB – 2 GB |
| Image storage requirement | $S_{\text{store}} \geq 2 \times S_{\text{src}}$ | bytes | Best practice |
| CRC-32 (E01 per segment) | $C = \text{CRC32}(S_i)$ | 32-bit integer | Detects single-bit errors |
| Compression ratio | $R_c = S_{\text{compressed}} / S_{\text{raw}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \lt R_c \leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These tools are deployed by **CERT-In (India)**, **Interpol**, and corporate SOCs. The E01 format's adoption ensures cross-tool compatibility — an image acquired in EnCase can be opened in Autopsy and FTK, supporting *tool-agnostic* investigation workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Stream Acquisition Integrity Chain

Let a source drive of size $S$ bytes be acquired into an E01 image with $n$ segments. We derive the integrity verification algorithm used by both EnCase and Autopsy.

**Step 1 — Source Hash Computation:**
The acquisition tool reads the source media sequentially and computes a rolling hash:

$$
H_{\text{src}} = \text{SHA-}1\!\left(\bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right)
$$

where $S_i$ is the $i$-th source segment of $S$, and $\bigoplus$ denotes sequential concatenation.

**Step 2 — Segment-Level Hash Storage:**
Each E01 segment stores its own hash $h_i$ and CRC $c_i$:

$$
h_i = \text{MD5}(S_i), \qquad c_i = \text{CRC32}(S_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Verification Phase:**
After acquisition, the examiner recomputes:

$$
h_i' = \text{MD5}(S_i') \quad \text{for each } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
$$

**Step 4 — Integrity Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{Status} = \begin{cases} \text{VERIFIED} & \text{if } h_i = h_i' \;\;\forall\, i \\ \text{FAILED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Composite Hash Check:**

$$
H_{\text{src}} = H_{\text{dst}} \implies \text{End-to-end integrity confirmed}
$$

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — EnCase vs Autopsy

| Feature Dimension | EnCase Forensic | Autopsy |
|---|---|---|
| **License** | Commercial (proprietary) | Open-source (GPL-2.0) |
| **Platform** | Windows only | Windows, Linux, macOS |
| **Underlying Engine** | Proprietary C++ engine | The Sleuth Kit (TSK) |
| **Default Image Format** | E01 (Expert Witness) | Raw (dd), E01 (via libewf), AFF |
| **GUI Style** | Native Windows (MFC-based) | Browser-based (HTML/JS) |
| **Multi-Examiner Cases** | Native support | Limited; needs network deployment |
| **Keyword Indexing** | Built-in fast indexer | Built-in Solr/Lucene indexer |
| **Mobile Forensics** | Via EnCase Neutrino add-on | Limited native; relies on plugins |
| **Cloud Forensics** | EnCase Cloud Investigator module | Manual via custom modules |
| **RAM Forensics** | Via separate Volatility integration | Native integration via Volatility plugin |
| **Reporting** | Templated HTML/PDF court reports | HTML reports; less court-formatted |
| **Cost** | High (per-examiner licensing) | **Free** |
| **Learning Curve** | Steep (industry training required) | Moderate (academic-friendly) |
| **Court Admissibility** | Widely accepted globally | Accepted when methodology is documented |

### 3.3 Decision Algorithm: Choosing the Right Tool

For a KTU-style analytical question, apply the following decision flow:

1. **Is the budget zero?** → Use **Autopsy**.
2. **Is multi-terabyte, multi-examiner enterprise case required?** → Use **EnCase**.
3. **Is mobile device forensics the primary need?** → Use **EnCase** with mobile add-on (or **Cellebrite** for advanced cases).
4. **Is the deliverable a court-grade PDF report?** → Use **EnCase**.
5. **Is the requirement an academic / lab demonstration?** → Use **Autopsy**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EnCase Forensic — End-to-End Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Media Disk] -->|Connected via Hardware Write-Blocker| B[EnCase Examiner GUI]
    B --> C{Acquire Bit-Stream}
    C -->|E01 Format| D[Evidence File with MD5 and SHA1 Hash]
    D --> E[Verify Hash Integrity]
    E -->|Pass| F[Mount as Read-Only Case File]
    E -->|Fail| G[Re-Acquire Evidence]
    F --> H[Evidence Processor Module]
    H --> I[File System Analysis]
    H --> J[Registry and Log Parsing]
    H --> K[Keyword and Indexed Search]
    I --> L[Bookmarking Suspect Artifacts]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Generate Court-Ready Report]
    M --> N[Chain-of-Custody Documentation]
```

### 4.2 Autopsy — Three-Layer Architectural Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[UI Layer - Browser Based GUI]
        UI1[Case Dashboard]
        UI2[Tree Viewer]
        UI3[Content Viewer]
        UI4[Timeline View]
    end

    subgraph MID[Core Processing Layer - Java Backend]
        MID1[Ingest Module Manager]
        MID2[Solr Keyword Indexer]
        MID3[Hash Database Engine]
        MID4[Report Generator]
    end

    subgraph BOT[Low-Level Engine - The Sleuth Kit TSK]
        BOT1[mmls - Volume Parser]
        BOT2[fsstat - File System Analyzer]
        BOT3[fls - File Lister]
        BOT4[icat - File Extractor]
        BOT5[libewf - E01 Reader]
    end

    UI1 --> MID1
    UI2 --> MID1
    UI3 --> MID2
    UI4 --> MID1
    MID1 --> BOT1
    MID1 --> BOT2
    MID2 --> BOT3
    MID3 --> BOT4
    MID4 --> BOT5
    BOT1 --> EVID[Raw dd Image or E01 Evidence File]
    BOT2 --> EVID
    BOT3 --> EVID
    BOT4 --> EVID
    BOT5 --> EVID
```

### 4.3 Hash Verification Process — Shared by Both Tools

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Media] -->|Compute| B[Hash Source]
    A -->|Acquire Bit-Stream| C[Evidence Image]
    C -->|Compute| D[Hash Image]
    B --> E{Compare Hash Values}
    D --> E
    E -->|Match| F[Evidence VERIFIED]
    E -->|Mismatch| G[Evidence CORRUPTED - Re-Acquire]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
*List any three key features of EnCase Forensic. Why is it considered the industry standard for digital investigations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

EnCase Forensic is a Windows-based commercial digital investigation suite developed by Guidance Software (now OpenText). Three key features are:

1. **Bit-stream image acquisition** in the proprietary **E01 (Expert Witness Format)** which stores both data and forensic metadata (hashes, CRC, case info) in a self-authenticating structure.
2. **Integrated case management** with multi-examiner collaboration, bookmarking, and full chain-of-custody logging.
3. **Court-grade reporting** with legally accepted HTML/PDF templates accepted in jurisdictions worldwide.

It is the industry standard because its evidence files have been tested and upheld in courts across multiple jurisdictions, it is deployed by FBI/Interpol/law-enforcement globally, and its workflow enforces strict evidentiary integrity (1 mark each, plus concluding sentence).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
*Differentiate between the underlying forensic engines of EnCase and Autopsy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EnCase | Autopsy |
|---|---|---|
| Engine | Proprietary C++ engine | Open-source **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** |
| Interface | Native Windows MFC GUI | Browser-based HTML/JS GUI |
| Source | Closed, licensed by OpenText | Open-source (GPL-2.0), Basis Technology |

Autopsy is essentially a GUI wrapper over TSK, whereas EnCase uses a fully proprietary stack. This makes Autopsy free, extensible, and platform-independent, while EnCase offers tighter integration and enterprise-grade case management (any 3 differentiating points × 1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of **EnCase Forensic** with a neat block diagram. List its major components and their roles in a digital investigation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **E01 Evidence File Format** in detail. Why is segmentation done, and how is integrity maintained? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Architecture of EnCase (7 marks):**

EnCase Forensic follows a **layered modular architecture** consisting of:

1. **EnCase Examiner (GUI Layer):** The investigator's interface for opening cases, viewing files, searching, and bookmarking evidence. **(1 mark)**

2. **Acquisition Module:** Performs bit-stream imaging of source media through a write-blocker, producing E01 image files. **(1 mark)**

3. **Evidence Processor:** Automates extraction of artifacts — registry hives, internet history, email archives, installed programs. **(1 mark)**

4. **Analysis Engine:** Provides file system traversal, indexed keyword search, hash matching against known-bad databases, and file carving. **(1 mark)**

5. **Reporting Module:** Generates court-formatted HTML/PDF reports with chain-of-custody audit trails. **(1 mark)**

6. **Database Layer:** Stores case metadata, bookmark entries, and search indexes in an internal database. **(1 mark)**

7. **Write-Protection Subsystem:** Works with hardware write-blockers to ensure source media is never modified. **(1 mark)**

[Refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram** for the visual schematic to be drawn in the exam hall.]

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b) — E01 Evidence File Format (7 marks):**

The **Expert Witness Format (E01 / EWFB)** is a proprietary but open-documented container format used by EnCase to package forensic images.

**Structure of an E01 File:**
- **Header Segment:** Contains case number, examiner name, acquisition date/time, notes, and a unique evidence identifier. **(1 mark)**
- **Data Segments:** The actual bit-stream data, split into segments of configurable size (default **640 MB**). Each segment is stored as a separate physical file (`image.E01`, `image.E02`, `image.E03`, …). **(2 marks)**
- **Per-Segment Hash Table:** Each segment stores its **MD5 hash** and a **CRC-32 checksum**. **(1 mark)**
- **Footer:** Contains the composite **SHA-1 hash** of the entire image and acquisition metadata. **(1 mark)**

**Why Segmentation?** **(1 mark)**
- Circumvents the **2 GB / 4 GB file-system size limit** on older FAT32/legacy systems.
- Enables parallel hash computation and faster verification.
- Allows partial recovery if one segment is corrupted.

**How Integrity is Maintained:** **(1 mark)**
- On acquisition, hashes are computed and stored inside the E01.
- On verification, the tool recomputes MD5 per segment and SHA-1 of the whole image; if any segment hash mismatches, the tool flags the segment as corrupted and prompts re-acquisition.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **three-layer architecture of Autopsy** with its relationship to **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)**. List any four ingest modules and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **EnCase and Autopsy** across the dimensions of licensing, platform support, evidence formats, and court-reporting capability. Which tool would you recommend for (i) an academic lab demonstration, and (ii) a high-stakes criminal trial? Justify your answers. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Autopsy Architecture (7 marks):**

Autopsy is structured in **three layers**:

1. **Top Layer — UI (Browser-based GUI):** HTML/JavaScript interface providing case dashboard, tree viewer, content viewer, and timeline view. Accessible via local browser on `localhost`. **(1 mark)**

2. **Middle Layer — Java Backend:** Core processing engine that orchestrates ingest modules, manages the Solr keyword index, performs hash database lookups, and generates reports. **(1 mark)**

3. **Bottom Layer — The Sleuth Kit (TSK):** A collection of C/C++ command-line tools that perform low-level forensic operations: **(1 mark)**
   - `mmls` — parses partition table (volume layout).
   - `fsstat` — reports file system statistics.
   - `fls` — lists files including deleted ones.
   - `icat` — extracts file contents by inode number.

**Four Ingest Modules:** **(4 × 1 mark = 4 marks)**

| Module | Function |
|---|---|
| **Recent Activity** | Extracts web history, USB device connections, recent documents |
| **Hash Lookup** | Compares file hashes against NSRL known-good database to flag unknown files |
| **File Type Identification** | Uses libmagic to detect MIME types independent of file extensions |
| **Keyword Search** | Performs indexed and regex-based searches across all extracted content |

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Dimension | EnCase | Autopsy |
|---|---|---|
| **Licensing** | Proprietary, paid | Open-source, free |
| **Platform** | Windows only | Windows, Linux, macOS |
| **Evidence Formats** | E01 (native), raw (read-only) | Raw/dd (native), E01 (via libewf), AFF |
| **Court Reporting** | Templated, court-tested PDF/HTML | HTML reports, less formal court formatting |

**Recommendation:**

**(i) Academic Lab Demonstration → Autopsy.** Because it is **free, cross-platform, and uses the well-documented TSK backend**, students can install it without licensing cost, experiment freely, and understand the underlying UNIX forensic commands. Open-source also enables custom plug-in development for projects. **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) High-Stakes Criminal Trial → EnCase.** Because its **E01 format has been upheld in courtrooms globally**, its reports follow legally vetted templates, its chain-of-custody audit logs are exhaustive, and law-enforcement agencies worldwide recognize and trust its methodology. **(1.5 marks)**

[Allocate remaining 4 marks across the comparison table rows and justification logic.]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *EnCase* (forensics tool) with *EnCase eDiscovery* (a separate legal-review product by OpenText). Examiners deduct marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Do not** state that Autopsy "cannot open E01 files" — Autopsy reads E01 via the `libewf` library since version 3.0. Older answers in textbooks may be outdated.
> 3. **Do not** forget to mention **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** when describing Autopsy — examiners treat omission of TSK as a missing key concept.
> 4. **Do not** present the write-blocker as optional — it is *mandatory* for source-media protection and is a frequently tested concept.
> 5. **Do not** write only the hash equation without explaining the verification flow — KTU expects both the formula *and* the procedural narrative.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EnCase** = commercial, Windows-only, E01 native, court-grade reporting, industry standard.
- **Autopsy** = open-source, cross-platform, GUI over The Sleuth Kit, academically popular.
- **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** = the C/C++ command-line engine that powers Autopsy (`fls`, `icat`, `mmls`, `fsstat`).
- **E01 format** = segmented (default 640 MB), self-authenticating, stores MD5 + CRC32 + SHA-1.
- **Hash integrity equation:** $H_{\text{src}} \equiv H_{\text{dst}}$ — must hold for admissible evidence.
- **Standard hash digests:** MD5 = 128 bits, SHA-1 = 160 bits, SHA-256 = 256 bits.
- **Write-blocker** = mandatory hardware to prevent accidental write to source media.
- **Storage rule:** keep at least $2\times$ the source-disk size for image + working copy.
- **Ingest modules** in Autopsy include Recent Activity, Hash Lookup, File Type ID, Keyword Search, Exif Parser, and Email Parser.
- **EnCase components:** Examiner, Acquisition Module, Evidence Processor, Reporting Module.
- **Cross-tool compatibility:** An E01 image acquired in EnCase can be opened in Autopsy and vice-versa — interoperability is a key KTU conceptual point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies: Real-World Cybercrime Investigations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Real-World Cybercrime Investigations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cybercrime Investigation Case Study** is a documented, post-event forensic reconstruction of an actual criminal incident involving computer systems, networks, or digital artifacts, examined through the systematic application of **digital forensic methodologies**. It chronicles the sequence of events, evidentiary artefacts, investigative techniques, legal considerations, and lessons learned, serving as both an empirical reference and a pedagogical instrument.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A case study in cyber-forensics is *not* merely a news report. It is an **evidentiary reconstruction** that maps the **attack vector**, the **attacker actions (TTPs)**, the **system impact**, and the **forensic methodology** used to preserve, identify, analyze, and present digital evidence in a manner admissible under the **Indian IT Act 2000 / 2008 Amendment** and the **Indian Evidence Act 1872 (Section 65B)**.

## 1.2 Core Investigative Domains in Cyber Forensics

A cybercrime case study spans five overlapping investigative verticals:

| Domain | Forensic Sub-Type | Primary Evidence Source |
|---|---|---|
| Computer Forensics | Host-based analysis | Hard drives, RAM, OS logs |
| Network Forensics | Traffic/packet analysis | PCAP files, IDS/IPS logs, firewall logs |
