## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain the architecture of Snort with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Detection Engine and Preprocessors in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Snort Architecture (3 marks):**
Snort is a modular, packet-driven IDS/IPS consisting of five sequential components:

1. **Packet Decoder** — Parses link-layer (Ethernet), network-layer (IPv4/IPv6, ARP, ICMP), and transport-layer (TCP, UDP) headers.
2. **Preprocessors** — Prepare packets for the detection engine by normalising, defragmenting, and reassembling.
3. **Detection Engine** — The brain. Applies rules using Aho-Corasick multi-pattern matching.
4. **Logging/Alerting Subsystem** — Records events based on the chosen output format.
5. **Output Plugins** — Dispatch alerts to Sguil, BASE, Syslog, unified2, or databases.

**2. Role of Preprocessors (2 marks):**
- `frag2` / `frag3` — Reassemble fragmented IP packets so attackers cannot bypass signatures by splitting payloads.
- `stream5` — Maintains TCP state (SYN, SYN-ACK, ESTABLISHED) to enable flow-based detection.
- `http_inspect` — Normalises HTTP URIs (decodes hex, strips double encoding) to defeat obfuscation.
- `sfportscan` — Detects port-scans (SYN, NULL, FIN, XMAS).
- `ARP spoof` — Detects ARP poisoning on LANs.

**3. Role of Detection Engine (2 marks):**
- Stores rules in a 2D linked-list (RuleListNode) categorised by action and protocol.
- Uses the **Aho-Corasick algorithm** for $O(n + m + z)$ multi-pattern content matching.
- Evaluates options in performance order: cheap checks first (`flags`, `dsize`), then `content`, finally `pcre` (most expensive).
- If all options in a rule match a packet, the engine triggers the rule's action (alert/drop).

**Q.A.(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Write a Snort rule to detect an SQL Injection attempt on an Apache web server. Explain each component of the rule.**

**Model Solution:**

```
alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET $HTTP_PORTS (msg:"SQL Injection - UNION SELECT attack detected"; flow:to_server,established; content:"UNION"; nocase; content:"SELECT"; nocase; distance:0; classtype:web-application-attack; sid:1000001; rev:1; reference:cve,2023-XXXXX;)
```

**Component Explanation (7 marks):**

| Component | Meaning | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `alert` | Action: generate an alert and log the packet | 0.5 |
| `tcp` | Protocol: inspect only TCP packets | 0.5 |
| `$EXTERNAL_NET any` | Source: any IP outside the protected network, any port | 0.5 |
| `->` | Direction: one-way (source to destination) | 0.5 |
| `$HOME_NET` | Destination: the protected network | 0.5 |
| `$HTTP_PORTS` | Destination port: typically 80 and 443 | 0.5 |
| `msg:"..."` | Human-readable alert message | 0.5 |
| `flow:to_server,established` | Match only on established sessions going to the server | 1.0 |
| `content:"UNION"; nocase;` | Match the literal string "UNION" case-insensitively | 1.0 |
| `content:"SELECT"; nocase; distance:0;` | Match "SELECT" starting from where the previous match ended | 1.0 |
| `classtype:web-application-attack` | Categorises the alert for priority | 0.5 |
| `sid:1000001` | Unique Snort Rule ID (>1,000,000 for local rules) | 0.5 |
| `reference:cve,2023-XXXXX` | Links to a CVE entry for context | 0.5 |

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.(a) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Describe the architecture of Suricata. How does it differ from Snort in terms of multi-threading and output format?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Suricata Architecture (3 marks):**
Suricata is a multi-threaded IDS/IPS/NSM engine with the following components:
- **Packet Source Layer** — Supports AF_PACKET, PF_RING, DPDK, NFQUEUE, IPFIX for high-speed capture.
- **Threading** — Multiple specialised threads (capture, decode, stream, detect workers, output).
- **Decoders** — Ethernet, IPv4/v6, TCP, UDP, ICMP, SCTP parsers.
- **Stream Engine** — TCP stream tracking and reassembly.
- **Application-Layer Parsers** — HTTP, TLS, DNS, FTP, SMTP, SMB, SSH, MODBUS, DNP3 — built-in, no preprocessor needed.
- **Detection Engine** — Multi-threaded, runs the same Aho-Corasick + PCRE pipeline as Snort.
- **Outputs (EVE)** — All events emitted as structured JSON to `eve.json`.

**2. Multi-Threading Difference (2 marks):**
- **Snort 2.9:** Single-threaded; cannot exploit multi-core CPUs.
- **Suricata:** Native multi-threading with runmodes (`workers`, `autofp`, `flowpin`) — scales linearly across CPU cores. CPU affinity pinning via `worker-cpuset`.
- **Snort 3:** Now multi-threaded, but Suricata still leads with mature threading.

**3. Output Format Difference (2 marks):**
- **Snort:** tcpdump binary, unified2 binary, Syslog, database via Barnyard2 — *not* natively structured.
- **Suricata:** **EVE-JSON** — every alert, HTTP transaction, DNS query, TLS handshake, flow record is one JSON object per line. Easily ingested by ELK, Splunk, Wazuh, Graylog for real-time analytics.

**Q.B.(b) [7 marks]** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare Snort and Suricata on the basis of (i) threading model, (ii) rule syntax, (iii) output format, (iv) HTTP/TLS parsing, (v) hardware acceleration, (vi) licensing, and (vii) typical use case.**

**Model Solution Table (7 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Parameter | Snort | Suricata |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Threading | Single-threaded (v2); multithreaded (v3) | Fully multi-threaded natively |
| (ii) Rule Syntax | Original (header + body, options) | Snort-compatible + extensions (`iprep`, `luajit`, `dataset`) |
| (iii) Output Format | tcpdump, unified2, Syslog, DB | EVE-JSON, unified2, Syslog (structured) |
| (iv) HTTP/TLS Parsing | Preprocessor (v2) / Inspector (v3) | Built-in deep HTTP/TLS parser (no preprocessor) |
| (v) Hardware Acceleration | Snort 3: Hyperscan regex | Hyperscan + DPDK kernel-bypass |
| (vi) Licensing | GPLv2 (engine free, VRT rules paid) | GPLv2 (engine free, ET Open rules free) |
| (vii) Typical Use | Enterprises with VRT subscription | SOCs, ELK pipelines, NSM, government, ICS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing NIDS with NIPS** — remember IDS is *passive*, IPS is *inline* and *blocks*. Markers deduct for not stating deployment mode.
> 2. **Forgetting the arrow `->` direction** in Snort rules — `any any -> $HOME_NET 80` is the correct syntax; writing `any any => $HOME_NET 80` is **wrong** and costs 1 mark.
> 3. **SID rule** — `sid` must be > 1,000,000 for **local/custom** rules. Using a low number like `sid:1;` triggers a fatal "duplicate sid" error.
> 4. **Forgetting `flow:to_server,established;`** in HTTP-based rules — without it, the rule will fire on the **server-to-client** response packets as well, generating false positives.
> 5. **Confusing preprocessor (Snort 2) with inspector (Snort 3)** — Snort 3 uses Lua-based inspectors, not preprocessors. Snort 2.x is still widely deployed for exam purposes.
> 6. **Missing `;` semicolons** in rule options — every option except the last must end with a semicolon. A missing semicolon causes the rule to be silently ignored.
> 7. **EVE-JSON is Suricata-specific** — Snort does NOT natively produce EVE-JSON. Confusing the two loses marks in comparison questions.

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Yield KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Notes — Memorise Before Exam:**

- ✅ **IDS = passive, alert-only, out-of-band** (SPAN/TAP). **IPS = active, blocking, inline**.
- ✅ **Snort's 3 modes** = Sniffer (`-v`), Logger (`-l`), **NIDS (`-c snort.conf`)** — the last is the exam-favourite.
- ✅ **Snort rule = Header (action proto src_ip src_port -> dst_ip dst_port) + Body (options in parentheses)**.
- ✅ **Rule actions** = `alert`, `log`, `pass`, `drop`, `reject`, `sdrop` — `drop`/`reject` are IPS-only.
- ✅ **Key rule options** = `msg`, `content`, `nocase`, `offset`, `depth`, `distance`, `within`, `pcre`, `flow`, `sid`, `classtype`, `reference`, `threshold`, `metadata`.
- ✅ **`sid` for local rules must be > 1,000,000** to avoid clashing with VRT rules.
- ✅ **Snort 2.x architecture** = Decoder → Preprocessors → Detection Engine → Logging → Output Plugins.
- ✅ **Snort 3 (Snort++)** uses **LuaJIT**, **multithreading**, **inspectors** instead of preprocessors, native **TLS inspection**.
- ✅ **Aho-Corasick algorithm** = $O(n + m + z)$ — multi-pattern search used by both engines for content matching.
- ✅ **Suricata = multi-threaded IDS/IPS/NSM** released 2010 by **OISF**, funded by US DHS.
- ✅ **Suricata runmodes** = `workers`, `autofp`, `flowpin`, `single`.
- ✅ **Suricata EVE-JSON** = structured JSON output, one event per line — ideal for **ELK, Splunk, Wazuh**.
- ✅ **Suricata rule extensions** over Snort: `iprep`, `luajit`, `dataset`, `tls.*`, `http.*`, `dns.*`, `ssh.*`, `smtp.*`, `flowbits:isset`.
- ✅ **Suricata hardware acceleration** = Hyperscan (regex) + DPDK (kernel-bypass packet I/O).
- ✅ **Emerging Threats (ET) ruleset** works with **both** Snort and Suricata.
- ✅ **Snort maintainer = Cisco Talos**; **Suricata maintainer = OISF**; **Both are GPLv2**.
- ✅ **Typical SOC pipeline:** Suricata → EVE-JSON → Filebeat/Logstash → Elasticsearch → Kibana.
- ✅ **Performance formula:** Multi-thread speedup $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/n}$ by Amdahl's Law.
- ✅ **Best practice for `flow:`** = always use `flow:to_server,established;` for HTTP-attack rules to avoid FPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Forensics Tools: Wireshark, tcpdump, NetFlow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Network Forensics Tools: Wireshark, tcpdump, NetFlow # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Network Forensics Tools — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Network Forensics Tools** are specialized software utilities used by forensic investigators to **capture, record, analyze, and reconstruct** network traffic for the purpose of identifying, investigating, and documenting cyber-attacks, data exfiltration, policy violations, or anomalous behavior. They operate across the layers of the TCP/IP stack and produce legally admissible evidence. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic belongs to **Module 2 — Network Forensics Investigation**, and the three canonical tools mandated by the syllabus are: | Tool | Type | Primary Use | |------|------|-------------| | **Wireshark** | Graphical (GUI) | Deep packet inspection (DPI) | | **tcpdump** | Command-line (CLI) | Lightweight live capture on servers | | **NetFlow** | Flow-based telemetry | Aggregate traffic analytics | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The CCTV Control Room of a Building** > > - **Wireshark** is like a **high-resolution 4K CCTV monitor with rewind, zoom, and frame-by-frame playback**. A security guard can see *every single handshake, every byte exchanged* between two people in the lobby. > - **tcpdump** is like a **handheld body camera** worn by the guard. It records the same footage but in a compact text log — perfect for a patrolling guard with no space for a monitor. > - **NetFlow** is like the **visitor entry register at the gate**. It doesn't record the conversation inside the building — only *who entered, when, how long they stayed, and which rooms they visited*. It summarizes, not records. --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Layer Constants The following metrics are universally applied across all three tools when measuring network forensic data: - **Packets per second (pps)** — unit of capture rate - **Bytes per second (Bps)** — unit of throughput (note: lowercase **b** = bits, uppercase **B** = bytes; **1 B = 8 b**) - **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)** - **Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** = **1500 bytes** (Ethernet default) - **Capture file format** — **`.pcap`** (libpcap) or **`.pcapng`** (PCAP Next Generation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Golden Rules of Network Forensics (KTU Board Examiner Expectation)** > 1. **Capture first, analyze later** — always save raw packets to disk (`.pcap`). > 2. **Chain of Custody** — every captured artifact must be hashed (typically **MD5** or **SHA-256**). > 3. **Time synchronization** — all devices must use **NTP** (Network Time Protocol) so timestamps align with UTC. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Wireshark — Theoretical Breakdown Wireshark (formerly *Ethereal*) is an **open-source packet analyzer** that uses the **libpcap** (Linux) or **WinPcap/Npcap** (Windows) library to place the network interface into **promiscuous mode**. ### Operational Logic Steps - **Step 1 — Promiscuous Mode Enablement:** The NIC accepts *all* frames traversing the wire, not only those destined for its MAC address. - **Step 2 — Decapsulation:** The binary frames are parsed through the **OSI/TCP-IP stack** (Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application). - **Step 3 — Display Filtering:** The forensic analyst applies **BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter)** syntax or Wireshark's display filter language to isolate specific traffic (e.g., `http.request.uri contains "password"`). - **Step 4 — Protocol Dissection:** Wireshark applies built-in **dissectors** to translate raw bytes into human-readable protocol fields. - **Step 5 — Export Objects:** Files transferred over **HTTP, SMB, FTP, TFTP** can be extracted directly from a `.pcap` file. > [!NOTE] > **Why Wireshark matters in forensics:** It produces *full-payload* evidence — you can read the actual email body, HTTP cookie, or DNS query. This is its **core strength** and the reason it is the gold standard for **deep packet inspection (DPI)**. --- ## 2.2 tcpdump — Theoretical Breakdown `tcpdump` is a **command-line packet analyzer** originally written by **Van Jacobson, Sally Floyd, and Steve McCanne** in 1987 at LBNL. It is the de-facto forensic tool on **Linux/Unix servers** because it does not require a graphical display (X11). ### Operational Logic Steps - **Step 1 — BPF Compilation:** The filter expression (e.g., `tcp port 80`) is compiled into a BPF pseudo-machine program pushed to the kernel. - **Step 2 — Kernel-Level Capture:** Packets matching the filter are copied from kernel space to user space (avoiding expensive full copies). - **Step 3 — ASCII/HEX Dump:** The packet contents are printed to **stdout** in a human-readable form, or written directly to a `.pcap` file via the `-w` flag. - **Step 4 — Post-Capture Analysis:** Output `.pcap` files can be reopened in Wireshark for GUI analysis — a common KTU lab workflow. ### Essential tcpdump Filters (High-Yield) | Filter Expression | Function | |-------------------|----------| | `tcpdump -i eth0` | Capture on interface `eth0` | | `tcpdump -w capture.pcap` | Save to file (raw) | | `tcpdump -r capture.pcap` | Read from file | | `tcpdump -nn` | Disable name resolution (faster, IP/port shown numerically) | | `host 192.168.1.10` | Capture traffic to/from a specific host | | `tcp port 443` | Capture HTTPS traffic only | | `src net 10.0.0.0/8` | Capture from a source subnet | | `greater 1000` | Capture packets larger than **1000 bytes** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse `tcpdump -r` (reads pcap) with `tcpdump -w` (writes pcap). The board examiner will deliberately test this with a one-liner in Part A. --- ## 2.3 NetFlow — Theoretical Breakdown **NetFlow** is a **network protocol** developed by **Cisco Systems** in 1996 (introduced on the Cisco 5000 series routers). It does **not** capture packets — it records **flow records** as a summary metadata. ### The NetFlow 5-Tuple (The "Unidirectional Key") A **flow** is uniquely identified by these **5 fields**: $$ \text{Flow Key} = \{ \text{Source IP}, \text{Destination IP}, \text{Source Port}, \text{Destination Port}, \text{Protocol} \} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — Water Meter:** A water meter at a building records *total liters consumed* but doesn't record the individual drops. NetFlow records *total bytes/packets per conversation* but doesn't record the individual packet contents. ### NetFlow Architecture (3 Components) - **Exporter** — the router/switch that generates flow records (enabled via `ip flow export` on Cisco IOS). - **Collector** — a server (e.g., **NFdump, nfdump, SIEM**) that receives records via **UDP port 2055**. - **Analyzer** — software that visualizes the collected flows (e.g., **Flowalyzer, Nfsen, Elastiflow**). ### NetFlow Version Comparison (Board-Important) | Version | Year | Key Feature | |---------|------|-------------| | **v1** | 1996 | First release, 7-tuple key, no masks | | **v5** | 1998 | Most widely deployed, adds AS, interface, TCP flags | | **v7** | 2000 | Catalyst switch-specific | | **v9** | 2003 | Template-based, supports IPv6, MPLS, BGP next-hop | | **IPFIX** (v10) | 2008 | IETF standard (RFC 7011), based on v9 but extensible | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table — Use `\\vert` for absolute value bars to preserve markdown syntax.** | Concept | Formula | Units / Boundary | |---------|---------|------------------| | Capture rate | $\text{pps} = \dfrac{N_{packets}}{\Delta t}$ | packets/sec | | Throughput | $\text{Bps} = \dfrac{N_{bytes} \times 8}{\Delta t}$ | bits/sec (after $\times 8$) | | Capture file size | $S_{pcap} = H + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( L_i + P_i \right)$ | bytes, where $H$ = pcap global header (**24 B**), $L_i$ = packet header (**16 B**), $P_i$ = packet payload | | File-size to disk | $S_{disk} \approx S_{pcap}$ | bytes | | Packet size range | $64 \le P \le 1518$ | bytes (Ethernet) | | NetFlow cache expiry | Active timeout = **30 s**; Inactive timeout = **15 s** | seconds | | NetFlow record size | Fixed **48 bytes** (v5) or template-defined (v9) | bytes | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wireshark** → used by **SOC analysts, incident responders, malware reverse-engineers**; integrated with tools like **Zeek, Suricata, NetworkMiner**. - **tcpdump** → used in **containerized environments (Docker/Kubernetes)** where no GUI is available; standard pre-installed on most Linux distros. - **NetFlow** → used by **ISPs, NOCs, large enterprises** for **capacity planning, billing, anomaly detection (e.g., DDoS, beaconing, data exfiltration)**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: NetFlow Record Size in Version 5 The **NetFlow v5 export packet** has a fixed structure. We derive the total record size step by step. ### Given: - NetFlow v5 export packet contains **1 header + N flow records**. - Header size = **24 bytes** (fixed). - Each flow record size = **48 bytes** (fixed in v5). ### Step 1 — Express total size of a NetFlow export packet $$ S_{total} = H_{v5} + N \cdot R_{v5} $$ Substituting the known values: $$ S_{total} = 24 + N \cdot 48 $$ ### Step 2 — Express the number of records per UDP datagram Since NetFlow v5 uses UDP, the maximum payload before fragmentation is the **Ethernet MTU minus headers**: $$ L_{max} = 1500 - 20_{IP} - 8_{UDP} = 1472 \text{ bytes} $$ The number of records fitting in one export packet: $$ N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{1472 - 24}{48} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1448}{48} \right\rfloor $$ Evaluating the division: $$ \frac{1448}{48} = 30.166\ldots $$ Taking the floor (integer constraint — UDP cannot fragment NetFlow): $$ N_{max} = 30 \text{ records per packet} $$ ### Step 3 — Total bytes per export packet at maximum capacity $$ S_{max} = 24 + 30 \cdot 48 = 24 + 1440 = 1464 \text{ bytes} $$ This sits safely under the 1472-byte UDP payload boundary — no IP fragmentation occurs. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is *why* NetFlow is bandwidth-efficient. Even at peak load, an exporter only emits **~1.5 KB per batch** of 30 conversations. For 100,000 flows/min, the bandwidth overhead is **~240 kbps** — negligible on a 1 Gbps link. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tool Selection Decision Tree Use the following matrix to identify the correct tool for a given forensic scenario — a **favourite 14-mark KTU question pattern**. | Scenario | Best Tool | Why? | |----------|-----------|------| | Extract an HTTP file (malware sample) from a `.pcap` | **Wireshark** | Full-payload capture; GUI *Export Objects* feature | | Live-capture packets on a remote headless Linux server | **tcpdump** | CLI-based, low overhead, no X11 required | | Detect a port-scan attack across an enterprise | **NetFlow** | Aggregate view of thousands of flows shows scanner's pattern | | Reassemble a TCP session (FTP file transfer) | **Wireshark** | "Follow TCP Stream" feature reconstructs full session | | Forensic capture inside a Docker container | **tcpdump** | No GUI; standard `apt-get install tcpdump` | | Volume-of-traffic analysis (top talkers, top apps) | **NetFlow** | Built-in NetFlow collectors like **nfdump** rank by bytes/flows | | Capture 100 GB of traffic for offline analysis | **tcpdump + ring buffer** (`-W 10 -C 100`) | Stable, low-CPU, prevents disk fill | | Find a covert DNS tunnel | **Wireshark** | Need to inspect **DNS query payload** (TXT records with base64) | | Billing a customer by bandwidth usage | **NetFlow** | Per-flow byte counters; ideal for metered billing | | Find a specific TLS cipher downgrade | **Wireshark** | Inspect **ClientHello / ServerHello** packets | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: tcpdump BPF Filter Compilation Given a filter expression: `tcp and src host 10.0.0.5 and dst port 80` ### Step 1 — Lexical Analysis The filter is tokenized into: - `tcp` → protocol keyword - `src host 10.0.0.5` → qualifier - `dst port 80` → qualifier ### Step 2 — BPF Pseudo-Machine Translation The BPF is a register machine. The expression is compiled into a sequence of **load**, **jump**, and **return** instructions. ### Step 3 — Kernel Hook Attachment The compiled BPF program is attached to the **packet socket** via `setsockopt()`. When a packet arrives in kernel space, the BPF program decides whether to copy it to user space. ### Step 4 — Capture Decision $$ \text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{COPY} & \text{if BPF returns } N > 0 \\ \text{DROP} & \text{if BPF returns } 0 \end{cases} $$ This kernel-level filtering is what makes `tcpdump` **orders of magnitude faster** than userspace filters. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 NetFlow Architecture — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Router Interface
Ethernet 0/0] --> B[NetFlow Exporter
Cisco IOS] B --> C[UDP Port 2055
NetFlow v5] C --> D[Collector Server
nfdump daemon] D --> E[Flow Storage
MySQL Backend] E --> F[Analyzer GUI
Nfsen Web UI] F --> G[Forensic Report
PDF Export] H[NTP Server] --> B H --> D I[Admin / SOC Analyst] --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Packets enter the router on `Eth0/0` → router classifies them into the flow cache (keyed by 5-tuple) → exports aggregated records every **30 s** (active timeout) over UDP/2055 → collector stores them → analyst visualizes in Nfsen. **NTP** is shared by exporter and collector to keep timestamps aligned. --- ## 4.2 Wireshark Capture Pipeline — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Network Wire
Ethernet Frame] --> P2[NIC Driver
Promiscuous Mode] P2 --> P3[libpcap / Npcap
Kernel Buffer] P3 --> P4[BPF Filter
Display Layer] P4 --> P5[Protocol Dissector
TCP UDP DNS HTTP] P5 --> P6[GUI Three Pane View
Packet List Details Bytes] P6 --> P7[Export Objects
Files Reassembly] P7 --> P8[PCAP File
Disk Storage] P8 --> P9[Follow TCP Stream
Session Reconstruction] ``` --- ## 4.3 Tool Comparison — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Do you need
full packet payload?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{GUI available?} Q1 -->|No| N1[Use NetFlow] Q2 -->|Yes| W1[Use Wireshark] Q2 -->|No| T1[Use tcpdump] W1 --> O1[Export to .pcap] T1 --> O2[Save with -w flag] O1 --> O3[Offload to Wireshark
for offline analysis] O2 --> O3 N1 --> O3 ``` --- ## 4.4 Forensic Investigation Workflow (Integrated View) ```mermaid flowchart LR I1[Incident Detected] --> I2[Identify Scope] I2 --> I3[Capture Live Traffic
tcpdump -i any -w] I3 --> I4[Open in Wireshark
Apply Display Filter] I4 --> I5[Extract IOCs
IPs Hashes URLs] I5 --> I6[Cross-reference
NetFlow Records] I6 --> I7[Correlate Timeline
UTC Timestamps] I7 --> I8[Generate Forensic Report
Chain of Custody] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Differentiate between Wireshark and tcpdump in terms of architecture, platform, and forensic use case.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Wireshark | tcpdump | |-----------|-----------|---------| | Type | GUI (Graphical) | CLI (Command-line) | | Platform | Windows, Linux, macOS | Linux, Unix, BSD (servers) | | Resource Use | Heavy (needs GUI) | Lightweight | | Primary Use | Deep packet inspection, GUI analysis | Live capture on remote/headless servers | | Output | Visual 3-pane view + pcap | ASCII/HEX on stdout or `.pcap` via `-w` | | Forensic Role | Detailed analysis, evidence extraction | Initial capture at the wire | **Mark Split:** 1 mark architecture + 1 mark platform + 1 mark forensic use case. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **List the five fields that form the NetFlow 5-tuple key. Why is this tuple considered unidirectional?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The NetFlow v5 flow key consists of: 1. **Source IP Address** (e.g., `10.0.0.5`) 2. **Destination IP Address** (e.g., `93.184.216.34`) 3. **Source Port Number** (e.g., `49152`) 4. **Destination Port Number** (e.g., `443`) 5. **Layer-4 Protocol** (e.g., `TCP = 6`, `UDP = 17`) **Unidirectionality Reason (1 mark):** A *single* conversation between two hosts (e.g., a request and its reply) actually produces **two separate NetFlow records** — one for the request direction, one for the reply. The source and destination fields are *swapped*, so the 5-tuple differs. Hence a flow is **unidirectional** by definition. The reverse direction becomes a *new* flow record. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 2 Compulsory]** **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **NetFlow architecture**. List the three main components and describe the role of the **Exporter** with the help of its internal flow-cache mechanism. **(b) [7 marks]** A company observes a sudden spike in outbound traffic. How would you use **NetFlow records** to detect a potential **data exfiltration** attack? List the specific fields you would query. #### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a) **NetFlow Architecture Diagram (reproduced for exam — write freehand):** ``` [Router] --(UDP 2055)--> [Collector] --(TCP)--> [Analyzer] | | | Flow Cache nfdump daemon Nfsen Web UI ``` **Three Main Components (3 marks):** 1. **Exporter** — Lives on the router/switch. Maintains a **flow cache** in memory. When a new packet arrives: - It checks if a matching 5-tuple already exists in the cache. - If yes → updates packet/byte counters. - If no → creates a new flow entry. - When the flow expires (active timeout = 30 s, or inactive = 15 s), the record is **exported** to the collector via UDP. 2. **Collector** — A host running `nfcapd` (from the `nfdump` toolset) listening on **UDP/2055**. It writes the records to rotating files (default: 5-minute bins). 3. **Analyzer** — A GUI such as **Nfsen** that graphs flows over time, ranks top talkers, and allows filtering by IP/port/AS. **Flow Cache Mechanism (4 marks):** The cache is a **hash table** keyed by the 5-tuple. Each entry contains: - Packet count, byte count - Start timestamp, end timestamp - Input/output interface, TCP flags, Next-hop IP, AS numbers The router uses **Sampled NetFlow** (default 1-in-1) to reduce CPU load on high-speed links. **Mark Split:** 3 marks diagram+components + 4 marks flow cache explanation. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b) **Using NetFlow to Detect Data Exfiltration (7 marks):** 1. **Identify Top Talkers (2 marks):** Run `nfdump -s srcip/bytes` to rank internal hosts by total outbound bytes. Anomalous spikes (e.g., 50 GB at 2 AM) suggest exfiltration. 2. **Long-Duration Flows (2 marks):** Run `nfdump -s duration` to find flows lasting hours — typical of slow data leaks via HTTP/HTTPS. 3. **Destination IP Reputation (1 mark):** Cross-reference destination IPs with threat-intel feeds (e.g., AbuseIPDB) to flag connections to known malicious hosts. 4. **Unusual Port Use (1 mark):** Filter `nfdump 'dst port not 80 and dst port not 443'` to find data leaving on non-standard ports. 5. **Beaconing Detection (1 mark):** Look for periodic small flows to the same external IP (typical of C2 beaconing). **Specific Fields to Query (free text):** `src_ip`, `dst_ip`, `dst_port`, `bytes`, `packets`, `duration`, `tcp_flags`, `next_hop`. --- ### **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **promiscuous mode** operation required for packet capture. How does **Wireshark** decapsulate an Ethernet frame? List the five protocol layers (from data link to application) as Wireshark displays them. **(b) [7 marks]** Using the formula $S_{total} = 24 + N \cdot 48$, calculate the **maximum number of NetFlow v5 records per export packet** such that no IP fragmentation occurs on a standard Ethernet link (MTU = 1500). Show each step. #### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a) **Promiscuous Mode (3 marks):** By default, a NIC only accepts frames whose **destination MAC address** matches its own. In **promiscuous mode** (enabled via `ifconfig eth0 promisc` or via Wireshark's capture options), the NIC accepts **every frame on the wire**, regardless of destination MAC. This is essential for a forensic analyst to see traffic between *other* hosts on the same collision domain (e.g., a hub or tap). **Wireshark Decapsulation (4 marks):** When an Ethernet frame enters Wireshark, it is parsed top-down through these **5 layers** (as displayed in the GUI): 1. **Frame** (Layer 1 — Physical, raw bits + capture metadata: timestamp, length) 2. **Ethernet II** (Layer 2 — Data Link, src/dst MAC, EtherType) 3. **Internet Protocol Version 4** (Layer 3 — Network, src/dst IP, TTL, flags) 4. **Transmission Control Protocol** (Layer 4 — Transport, src/dst port, seq, ack, flags) 5. **Hypertext Transfer Protocol** (Layer 7 — Application, GET / POST, headers, body) --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b) **Step-by-step derivation (already detailed in Section 3.1 — reproduce it):** **Step 1:** Write the size formula: $$ S_{total} = 24 + N \cdot 48 $$ **Step 2:** Compute the maximum UDP payload that fits inside an Ethernet frame without IP fragmentation: $$ L_{max} = 1500_{MTU} - 20_{IPheader} - 8_{UDPheader} = 1472 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3:** Set $S_{total} \le L_{max}$ and solve for $N$: $$ 24 + 48N \le 1472 $$ $$ 48N \le 1472 - 24 = 1448 $$ $$ N \le \frac{1448}{48} $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the division: $$ \frac{1448}{48} = 30.166\ldots $$ **Step 5:** Take the floor (integer constraint): $$ N_{max} = 30 \text{ records per packet} $$ **Step 6:** Verify by computing total size: $$ S_{max} = 24 + 30 \cdot 48 = 24 + 1440 = 1464 \text{ bytes} \le 1472 \text{ bytes} \quad \checkmark $$ **Final Answer:** **30 NetFlow v5 records** can be packed into a single export packet without IP fragmentation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Conflating Wireshark with tcpdump's "live" capability** — both can capture live, but only Wireshark has the GUI for follow-stream. Students who answer "Wireshark is for live, tcpdump is for offline" lose 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the 5-tuple in NetFlow** — the protocol field is **mandatory**; many students list only 4 fields and lose 1 mark in Part A. > 3. **Wrong active/inactive timeout values** — the standard values are **30 s active / 15 s inactive**, *not* 60/30. Markers deduct 1 mark for this. > 4. **Pcap header size error in derivation** — the libpcap global header is **24 bytes** and the per-packet header is **16 bytes**. Confusing these gives wrong file-size estimates. > 5. **Saying NetFlow captures packets** — it does **NOT**. NetFlow only exports *flow summaries*. Examiners specifically deduct 2 marks for this misconception. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Wireshark** = GUI, full-payload DPI, uses libpcap/Npcap, supports BPF + display filters. - [x] **tcpdump** = CLI, lightweight, uses BPF at kernel level; `-w` writes, `-r` reads, `-i` selects interface. - [x] **NetFlow** = flow-summary telemetry (NOT full packets), developed by **Cisco**, exports on **UDP/2055**. - [x] **NetFlow 5-tuple** = `src_ip, dst_ip, src_port, dst_port, protocol` — defines a *unidirectional* flow. - [x] **NetFlow v5** = 24-byte header + 48-byte fixed records; **30 records per packet** (no IP fragmentation). - [x] **Active timeout = 30 s**, **Inactive timeout = 15 s** (memorize these values). - [x] **Promiscuous mode** = NIC accepts all frames on the wire — required for forensic capture. - [x] **Decapsulation** = Frame → Ethernet → IP → TCP/UDP → Application (5 layers in Wireshark). - [x] **MTU = 1500 bytes** (Ethernet); max UDP payload for NetFlow = **1472 bytes**. - [x] **Pcap global header = 24 B**; per-packet header = **16 B**. - [x] **Chain of Custody** = every forensic artifact must be hashed (MD5/SHA-256). - [x] **NTP synchronization** = mandatory for timestamp correlation across exporter and collector. - [x] **IPFIX = IETF standard** (NetFlow v10) — based on v9, fully template-based and extensible. - [x] **Tool selection rule of thumb:** Wireshark = deep analysis | tcpdump = remote/headless capture | NetFlow = enterprise-wide traffic trends. - [x] **Export Objects** in Wireshark can extract files transferred over **HTTP, FTP, SMB, TFTP** directly from pcap. - [x] **Forensic workflow:** tcpdump (capture) → Wireshark (analyze) → NetFlow (correlate) → Report.

topics.title: Case Studies: Network Breach Investigations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Network Breach Investigations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Breach Investigation (NBI)** is the systematic, methodical, and legally admissible process of identifying, preserving, analyzing, and interpreting digital evidence derived from network devices, traffic logs, and endpoint telemetry to reconstruct the lifecycle of a cyber intrusion — from initial reconnaissance and exploitation to lateral movement, data exfiltration, and persistence.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this is formally classified under the **Incident Response (IR)** and **Digital Forensics** umbrella, conforming to the **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** Computer Security Incident Handling Guide and ISO/IEC 27035 standards.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Lexicon for KTU Board Examinations**
> - **IoC (Indicators of Compromise):** Forensic artifacts (IP addresses, file hashes, domain names, registry keys) that indicate a system has been breached.
> - **TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures):** The behavioral pattern of an attacker — the *how* and *why* of an attack.
> - **C2 (Command and Control):** The server/channel used by an attacker to maintain communication with compromised systems.
> - **Lateral Movement:** The technique used by attackers to pivot through a network after initial compromise.
> - **Exfiltration:** The unauthorized transfer of data from the victim's environment to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
> - **Chain of Custody:** The documented, unbroken trail of accountability for digital evidence from collection to court.
> - **Dwell Time:** The duration (typically measured in **days**) an attacker remains undetected within a network.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Heist Investigation"

Imagine a sophisticated bank heist. The vault is cracked open, cash is missing, and the police arrive. They cannot simply guess who did it. They must:
1. **Secure the crime scene** (preserve volatile logs, memory dumps, network state).
2. **Interview witnesses** (analyze log files, SIEM alerts, IDS/IPS records).
3. **Trace the getaway route** (reconstruct network traffic using NetFlow, PCAP, and firewall logs).
4. **Identify the tool marks** (malware hashes, exploit signatures, TTPs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK).
5. **Build a timeline** (correlate events across UTC timestamps from multiple sources).

A **Network Breach Investigation** is the *digital twin* of this forensic process — applied to packets, logs, and binaries.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters (Engineering Relevance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance**
> According to the IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024, the **global average breach cost is $4.88 million**, and the **average dwell time is 194 days**. For KTU graduates entering SOC analyst, incident response, or forensic consultant roles, mastery of breach investigation methodology is a *non-negotiable competency*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Phase NIST Incident Response Lifecycle

The **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2** framework divides every network breach investigation into six phases. KTU examiners frequently test this in **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

#### Phase 1 — Preparation
- Deploy SIEM, IDS/IPS, honeypots, and log aggregation.
- Maintain updated playbooks and contact trees.
- Pre-stage forensic tools (Wireshark, Volatility, Autopsy, Velociraptor).

#### Phase 2 — Detection & Analysis
- Triangulate alerts from **EDR**, **firewall**, **DNS logs**, and **proxy logs**.
- Determine **scope**: Which systems? Which users? Which data?
- Calculate the **attack vector** (phishing, supply chain, zero-day, misconfiguration).

#### Phase 3 — Containment
- **Short-term:** Isolate affected hosts via VLAN segmentation, firewall ACLs, EDR quarantine.
- **Long-term:** Patch the root cause, rebuild compromised systems from golden images.

#### Phase 4 — Eradication
- Remove malware, webshells, backdoors, and persistence mechanisms (cron jobs, scheduled tasks, registry Run keys).
- Rotate all credentials (service accounts, API keys, certificates).

#### Phase 5 — Recovery
- Restore systems from verified clean backups.
- Monitor with heightened SOC visibility for **at least 30 days**.
- Validate against the **MITRE ATT&CK** framework to ensure no residual IoCs.

#### Phase 6 — Lessons Learned (Post-Incident)
- Author a **blameless post-mortem** report.
- Update detection rules, runbooks, and employee training.
- Disclose per regulatory timelines (GDPR: **72 hours**, IT Act 2000/2008 India: as directed by CERT-In).

### 2.2 The Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain (Defender's View)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain**
> A 7-stage model describing the phases of a targeted cyber attack:
> 1. **Reconnaissance** — OSINT, port scanning, email harvesting.
> 2. **Weaponization** — Crafting malware, pairing exploits with payloads.
> 3. **Delivery** — Phishing email, watering hole, USB drop.
> 4. **Exploitation** — Triggering the vulnerability (e.g., CVE exploitation).
> 5. **Installation** — Dropping webshells, backdoors, rootkits.
> 6. **Command & Control (C2)** — Establishing beaconing to attacker server.
> 7. **Actions on Objectives** — Data exfiltration, ransomware deployment, sabotage.

### 2.3 MITRE ATT&CK Framework (Adversary Behavior Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> The **MITRE ATT&CK** matrix is a curated knowledge base of adversary TTPs organized into 14 tactic categories (Enterprise matrix). For KTU exams, remember the core tactic flow: **Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Privilege Escalation → Defense Evasion → Credential Access → Discovery → Lateral Movement → Collection → Exfiltration → Impact**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Investigation Metrics)

| **Metric / Concept** | **Definition / Standard Value** | **Unit / Range** |
|---|---|---|
| MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) | Average time to identify a breach | Days (Industry avg: 194 days) |
| MTTR (Mean Time to Respond) | Average time to contain a breach | Hours (Industry avg: 73 days) |
| Dwell Time | Duration attacker remains undetected | Days |
| Chain of Custody | Unbroken evidence trail | Documentation artifact |
| Order of Volatility | Network state > RAM > Disk > Logs | RFC 3227 priority |
| Time Synchronization | Use UTC with NTP for correlation | Timestamp standard |
| First Responder Rule | Capture RAM before disk before logs | Volatility order |
| IOC Lifespan | Average usefulness of an IoC before rotation | 24 to 48 hours |

### 2.5 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227 — Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a breach is detected, evidence must be collected in this priority (most volatile first):
> 1. CPU registers, cache
> 2. Routing table, ARP cache, process table, kernel statistics, memory (RAM)
> 3. Temporary file systems
> 4. Disk storage
> 5. Remote logging and monitoring data
> 6. Physical configuration and network topology
> 7. Archival media (backups)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — A Case Study Framework

### 3.1 Case Study: The "ShadowFox" Enterprise Spear-Phishing Breach

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A mid-sized enterprise ("Acme Corp") detects anomalous outbound DNS traffic to a domain registered 6 days ago. Employees in the finance department report that their endpoints are running slowly. The following analytical walkthrough demonstrates how investigators reconstruct the breach.

### 3.2 Phase-by-Phase Reconstruction

#### Step 1 — Initial Detection (Triangulation)

The SOC analyst receives three correlated alerts:

$$\text{Alert}_1: \text{EDR detects PowerShell encoded command on FIN-WS-04}$$

$$\text{Alert}_2: \text{DNS logs show beaconing to hxxps://shadowfox-cdn[.]com every 60 seconds}$$

$$\text{Alert}_3: \text{Firewall logs show FIN-WS-04 contacting 185.220.101[.]47 on port 443}$$

The **correlation rule** (simplified) is:

$$\text{Incident} = (\text{Process Anomaly} \land \text{DNS Beaconing} \land \text{Egress to High-Risk IP})$$

#### Step 2 — Scope Determination

Investigators query Active Directory to enumerate the lateral blast radius:

$$\text{Compromised Hosts} = \{h \in \text{Hosts} \mid \text{EDR.Alert}(h) = \text{true}\}$$

Initial scope: **4 hosts in finance subnet (10.10.20.0/24)**. After deeper log analysis, the scope expands to **17 hosts** across two subnets due to **SMB-based lateral movement** using stolen NTLM hashes.

#### Step 3 — Containment Decision Matrix

The decision to isolate is governed by a **risk equation**:

$$R_{\text{isolate}} = (V_{\text{data}} \times P_{\text{exfil}}) - (C_{\text{downtime}} + L_{\text{evidence}})$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{data}}$ = Value of the data on the host
- $P_{\text{exfil}}$ = Probability of successful exfiltration
- $C_{\text{downtime}}$ = Business cost of taking host offline
- $L_{\text{evidence}}$ = Risk of losing volatile evidence

For FIN-WS-04: $R_{\text{isolate}}$ is positive → **Isolate immediately**.

#### Step 4 — Forensic Acquisition (Order of Volatility Applied)

Following **RFC 3227**, the responder captures:

$$\text{Acquisition Order} = \{\text{NetFlow} \rightarrow \text{RAM dump} \rightarrow \text{Disk image} \rightarrow \text{Event logs}\}$$

For RAM acquisition (using WinPMEM or Magnet RAM Capture):

$$\text{Hash}_{\text{verify}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{RAM.img}) = \text{d4e5f6...}$$

This hash is recorded in the chain of custody document:

$$\text{CoC Entry} = \{\text{Timestamp}, \text{Investigator}, \text{Hash}, \text{Tool}, \text{Storage}\}$$

#### Step 5 — Timeline Reconstruction (Super-Timeline)

Using **Plaso/log2timeline**, events are normalized to UTC:

$$\text{Timeline} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \text{Events}(t_i, \text{source}_i, \text{action}_i)$$

| **UTC Timestamp** | **Source** | **Event** | **Kill Chain Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2024-09-01 08:14:22 | Mail Gateway | Spear-phish delivered to finance@acme.com | Delivery |
| 2024-09-01 08:23:11 | EDR | winword.exe spawns mshta.exe | Exploitation |
| 2024-09-01 08:24:03 | EDR | Encoded PowerShell executed (Base64) | Installation |
| 2024-09-01 08:30:00 | DNS Log | First beacon to shadowfox-cdn[.]com | C2 |
| 2024-09-04 22:15:44 | Firewall | SMB lateral move to FIN-WS-08 | Lateral Movement |
| 2024-09-07 03:00:12 | Proxy | 2.3 GB upload to attacker IP | Exfiltration |

#### Step 6 — Attribution and IOC Extraction

The extracted IoCs are:

$$\text{IoCs} = \{\text{IP}_{185.220.101.47}, \text{Domain}_{\text{shadowfox-cdn[.]com}}, \text{SHA256}_{\text{loader.exe}}, \text{Mutex}_{\text{Global\\ShadowFox2024}}\}$$

These are cross-referenced with **MITRE ATT&CK**:

$$\text{Technique Mapping: T1566.001 (Spear-phishing Attachment) } \rightarrow \text{T1059.001 (PowerShell)} \rightarrow \text{T1071.001 (Web Protocols C2)} \rightarrow \text{T1021.002 (SMB Lateral)} \rightarrow \text{T1041 (Exfil over C2)}$$

#### Step 7 — Eradication and Recovery

$$\text{Eradication} = \{\text{Reimage}(17 \text{ hosts}), \text{Rotate Credentials}(412 \text{ accounts}), \text{Block IoCs in firewall/EDR/Proxy}\}$$

### 3.3 Comparative Case Study Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Famous Network Breaches to Remember for KTU 2024 Exams**

| **Breach** | **Year** | **Vector** | **Dwell Time** | **Key Forensic Lesson** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Target | 2013 | Phishing email to HVAC vendor | ~ 2 weeks | Third-party / supply chain risk |
| Sony Pictures | 2014 | Spear-phishing + wiper malware | Months | Destructive intent, attribution complexity |
| WannaCry | 2017 | EternalBlue SMB exploit (CVE-2017-0144) | Hours (worm) | Unpatched systems spread infection |
| NotPetya | 2017 | M.E.Doc software supply chain | Hours | Backup integrity is critical |
| SolarWinds / SUNBURST | 2020 | Supply chain trojanized Orion update | ~ 9 months | Code-signing trust abuse |
| Colonial Pipeline | 2021 | Compromised VPN password (no MFA) | ~ 1 month | Lack of MFA = single point of failure |
| Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) | 2021 | JNDI injection in Apache Log4j | Days | Open-source dependency risk |

### 3.4 Key Forensic Calculations (Common in KTU Numerical Questions)

#### Dwell Time Calculation

$$\text{Dwell Time} = T_{\text{detection}} - T_{\text{initial intrusion}}$$

For ShadowFox case:

$$T_{\text{dwell}} = \text{2024-09-07 03:00:12} - \text{2024-09-01 08:14:22} = 5 \text{ days}, 18 \text{ hours}, 45 \text{ minutes}$$

#### Indicator Expiry Risk (Threat Intel Lifecycle)

$$\text{IoC Relevance}(t) = \text{IoC}_{\text{baseline}} \times e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where $\lambda$ is the decay constant (typical: 0.05 per day for IP-based IoCs).

#### Network Log Integrity (Hash Chaining)

For tamper-evident log storage:

$$H_n = \text{SHA-256}(H_{n-1} \parallel \text{Log}_n \parallel \text{Timestamp}_n)$$

This creates a Merkle-tree-like chain ensuring forensic admissibility.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Network Breach Investigation Lifecycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preparation: SIEM, IR Playbooks, Tooling] --> B[Detection: SIEM Alert, EDR, IDS, User Report]
    B --> C[Analysis: Scope, Vector, TTPs, IoCs]
    C --> D[Containment: Isolate Hosts, Block C2]
    D --> E[Eradication: Remove Malware, Rotate Creds]
    E --> F[Recovery: Restore from Backup, Monitor 30 Days]
    F --> G[Lessons Learned: Post-Mortem, Update Defenses]
    G --> A
```

### 4.2 Forensic Acquisition Workflow (Order of Volatility)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[NetFlow and Live Connections] --> O2[RAM Dump using WinPMEM]
    O2 --> O3[Process and Service List]
    O3 --> O4[Disk Image using FTK or dd]
    O4 --> O5[Windows Event Logs and Registry]
    O5 --> O6[Backup Tapes and Archives]
```

### 4.3 Attack Kill Chain Mapped to Defender Actions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Reconnaissance] --> K2[Weaponization]
    K2 --> K3[Delivery: Phishing Email]
    K3 --> K4[Exploitation: CVE or User Click]
    K4 --> K5[Installation: Backdoor Implant]
    K5 --> K6[Command and Control Beacon]
    K6 --> K7[Lateral Movement via SMB]
    K7 --> K8[Exfiltration or Impact]
    D1[Defender: Threat Intel Monitoring] -.blocks.-> K1
    D2[Defender: Email Gateway and Sandbox] -.blocks.-> K3
    D3[Defender: EDR and Patch Management] -.blocks.-> K4
    D4[Defender: Application Whitelisting] -.blocks.-> K5
    D5[Defender: Egress Filtering and DNS Sinkhole] -.blocks.-> K6
    D6[Defender: Network Segmentation and MFA] -.blocks.-> K7
    D7[Defender: DLP and Anomaly Detection] -.blocks.-> K8
```

### 4.4 Chain of Custody Documentation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[Evidence Acquired] --> H1[Compute SHA-256 Hash]
    H1 --> L1[Log into Chain of Custody Form]
    L1 --> S1[Sealed in Anti-Static Bag with Tamper Seal]
    S1 --> T1[Transferred to Forensic Lab]
    T1 --> V1[Hash Verified on Arrival]
    V1 --> A1[Analysis on Forensic Workstation]
    A1 --> R1[Report Generated with Court-Admissible Format]
```

### 4.5 Case Study Architecture (ShadowFox Intrusion Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Attacker[Attacker C2 Server 185.220.101.47] -->|HTTPS Beacon| Host1[FIN-WS-04 Initial Victim]
    Host1 -->|SMB Lateral Move| Host2[FIN-WS-08]
    Host1 -->|SMB Lateral Move| Host3[FIN-WS-12]
    Host2 -->|WMI Execution| Host4[FIN-WS-15]
    Host3 -->|RDP| Host5[FIN-SRV-02 File Server]
    Host5 -->|FTP Upload 2.3 GB| Attacker
    Host4 -->|DNS Tunnel| Attacker
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define the term "Indicator of Compromise (IoC)" with three examples relevant to network breach investigations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** is a forensic artifact that signals a system or network has been breached by an attacker. It is used by SOC analysts and incident responders to detect malicious activity. Examples relevant to network breach investigations include:
1. **IP Addresses** — e.g., a known malicious C2 server IP observed in firewall logs.
2. **Domain Names** — e.g., a DGA-generated or typosquatting domain contacted by infected hosts.
3. **File Hashes** — e.g., the SHA-256 hash of a malware sample discovered on an endpoint.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for three valid examples.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. List the six phases of the NIST SP 800-61 Incident Response Lifecycle.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The six phases are:
1. **Preparation** — Pre-incident readiness (tools, training, playbooks).
2. **Detection and Analysis** — Identifying and triaging the incident.
3. **Containment, Eradication, and Recovery** — Limiting damage, removing the threat, and restoring operations.
4. **Post-Incident Activity (Lessons Learned)** — Documentation and improvement.

*(Note: 3 marks are typically awarded for accurately listing all six phases with a one-line description each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain. Map each of the 7 stages to a corresponding defender detection mechanism. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Lockheed Martin Cyber Kill Chain** is a 7-stage model that describes the phases of a targeted cyber attack from the adversary's perspective. It is widely used by defenders to align detection and response capabilities.

1. **Reconnaissance:** Attacker gathers target information via OSINT, port scanning. *Defender detection:* Threat intelligence feeds, honeypots, log monitoring of scan signatures.
2. **Weaponization:** Attacker pairs an exploit with a payload (e.g., malicious document + macro). *Defender detection:* Malware sandboxes (Cuckoo, Joe Sandbox), YARA rules.
3. **Delivery:** Attack delivered via email, USB, or web. *Defender detection:* Email gateways, URL filtering, attachment sandboxing.
4. **Exploitation:** Vulnerability is triggered. *Defender detection:* EDR behavioral analysis, exploit guard, vulnerability scanners.
5. **Installation:** Malware installs persistence (registry, scheduled tasks). *Defender detection:* EDR file integrity monitoring, autoruns analysis.
6. **Command and Control (C2):** Malware beacons outbound. *Defender detection:* DNS monitoring, NetFlow analysis, JA3/JA3S fingerprinting, egress filtering.
7. **Actions on Objectives:** Data theft, ransomware, sabotage. *Defender detection:* DLP, UEBA, anomaly-based SIEM correlation.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per stage mapping = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) With reference to a real-world case study (e.g., the 2017 WannaCry attack), explain how network forensics was used to investigate the breach. Discuss the attack vector, IoCs, and lessons learned. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Background:** WannaCry was a ransomware worm that exploited **EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144)**, a vulnerability in the Server Message Block (SMB) v1 protocol of Microsoft Windows.

**Attack Vector:** The worm used a leaked NSA exploit (EternalBlue) to propagate via TCP port 445 without user interaction.

**Network Forensic Methodology:**
- **PCAP Analysis:** Investigators used **Wireshark** to capture SMB traffic and identified the exploitation of the SMBv1 buffer overflow.
- **IoC Extraction:** Extracted the **killswitch domain** (iuqerfsodp9ifjaposdfjhgosurijfaewrwergwea.com) and the **Bitcoin wallet addresses**. The kill switch was a hardcoded domain; registering it by a security researcher stopped the spread.
- **Hashing:** The ransomware sample SHA-1 hash (`84c82835a5d21bbcf75a61706d8ab549`) was shared globally.
- **Lateral Spread Mapping:** NetFlow records showed exponential fan-out from initial victim to thousands of hosts within hours.

**Lessons Learned:**
1. **Patch Management:** Systems running unpatched Windows (especially Windows 7 and Server 2008) were vulnerable. Microsoft's MS17-010 patch had been released **2 months prior**.
2. **Network Segmentation:** Flat networks allowed the worm to spread unchecked.
3. **SMBv1 Deprecation:** The legacy protocol should have been disabled.
4. **Defense in Depth:** Firewalls blocking TCP/445 externally and internally would have contained the blast radius.

*(Allocation: 2 marks attack vector, 2 marks IoCs/forensic tools, 3 marks lessons learned.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Discuss the role of MITRE ATT&CK framework in network breach investigations. Explain any five tactics from the Enterprise matrix with one technique under each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **MITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge)** framework is a globally accessible knowledge base of adversary TTPs based on real-world observations. It is the *de facto* standard for breach investigation and threat intelligence mapping in modern SOCs.

**Five Tactics and Example Techniques:**

1. **Initial Access (TA0001):** *Technique T1566.001 — Spear-phishing Attachment.* Attacker delivers a weaponized document via targeted email.
2. **Execution (TA0002):** *Technique T1059.001 — PowerShell.* Adversary uses PowerShell scripts for stealthy code execution.
3. **Persistence (TA0003):** *Technique T1547.001 — Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder.* Malware achieves persistence by adding itself to autorun registry keys.
4. **Lateral Movement (TA0008):** *Technique T1021.002 — SMB/Windows Admin Shares.* Attacker uses stolen credentials to access remote shares.
5. **Exfiltration (TA0010):** *Technique T1041 — Exfiltration Over C2 Channel.* Data is uploaded to attacker infrastructure using the same C2 channel.

**Role in Investigations:**
- **Standardized vocabulary** for incident reports.
- **Gap analysis** to identify missing detection rules.
- **Threat-informed defense** — map attacker behavior to specific SIGMA/YARA rules.
- **Attribution support** — adversary groups have signature TTP combinations.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per tactic + technique = 5 marks, 2 marks for role.)*

---

**(b) Describe the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227. Why is it critical to follow this order during a live network breach investigation? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The **Order of Volatility** is a principle outlined in **RFC 3227 — "Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving"** that prescribes the sequence in which digital evidence should be collected from a live system, prioritized by how quickly the data is lost.

**Order (Most Volatile First):**
1. **CPU Registers, Cache** — Lost in microseconds when power is cut.
2. **Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics, Memory (RAM)** — Lost in seconds to minutes.
3. **Temporary File Systems (/tmp, pagefile.sys)** — Lost on reboot.
4. **Disk Storage** — Persists until wiped.
5. **Remote Logging and Monitoring Data** — Persists based on retention policy.
6. **Physical Configuration and Network Topology** — Persists long-term.
7. **Archival Media (Backups)** — Persists for years.

**Why It Is Critical:**

1. **Evidence Preservation:** Volatile data (active network connections, RAM-resident malware, encryption keys) is lost the moment the system is powered off. Capturing RAM first may yield decryption keys, process injections, and rootkit footprints.
2. **Legal Admissibility:** Following RFC 3227 demonstrates forensic rigor and is a recognized best practice in court.
3. **Attacker Counter-Forensics:** Sophisticated attackers plant logic bombs that wipe disks on shutdown. Capturing RAM and network state *before* shutdown is essential.
4. **Attack Reconstruction:** Live network connections reveal active C2 channels, exfiltration sessions, and lateral movement tunnels that disk forensics would miss.
5. **Operational Triage:** The responder can quickly assess the "blast radius" from RAM-resident data without taking systems offline unnecessarily.

**Practical Example:** In the 2014 Sony Pictures breach, responders captured RAM dumps of infected systems before rebooting — this revealed the **Backdoor.Destover** wiper's in-memory artifacts and the **DGA-generated C2 domains** that were not present on disk.

*(Allocation: 3 marks order, 4 marks criticality with example.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the Cyber Kill Chain with MITRE ATT&CK.** They are *complementary*, not interchangeable. The Kill Chain has 7 linear phases; ATT&CK has 14 non-linear tactic categories.
> 2. **Skipping the order of volatility.** Examiners *specifically* deduct marks if you say "first image the disk" without justifying RAM-first capture.
> 3. **Omitting chain of custody.** Any forensic answer without a mention of hash verification and documentation is considered incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Generic IoCs.** Saying "the IP was malicious" without specifying the **IP, port, protocol, timestamp, and direction** (inbound/outbound) is treated as a vague answer.
> 5. **Ignoring UTC timestamps.** KTU expects all forensic timelines in **UTC** for cross-system correlation.
> 6. **No mention of regulatory compliance.** Topper answers include GDPR (72 hours), IT Act 2000/2008 (India), and CERT-In directives for breach disclosure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Case Studies: Network Breach Investigations**

- **Definition:** Network Breach Investigation = systematic process of identifying, preserving, analyzing, and interpreting digital evidence from network sources to reconstruct an intrusion.
- **NIST IR Lifecycle (6 phases):** Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment, Eradication & Recovery → Post-Incident Activity. (Some texts split CER into three, making six total.)
- **Lockheed Martin Kill Chain (7 stages):** Recon → Weaponization → Delivery → Exploitation → Installation → C2 → Actions on Objectives.
- **MITRE ATT&CK:** 14 Enterprise tactics. Key flow: Initial Access → Execution → Persistence → Privilege Escalation → Defense Evasion → Credential Access → Discovery → Lateral Movement → Collection → Exfiltration → Impact.
- **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** CPU → RAM → Temp FS → Disk → Logs → Topology → Backups.
- **Chain of Custody:** Unbroken, hash-verified documentation trail. SHA-256 is the standard hashing algorithm.
- **Key IoCs:** IPs, domains, file hashes, mutexes, registry keys, JA3 fingerprints.
- **Must-Know Case Studies:** WannaCry (2017, EternalBlue, SMB), NotPetya (2017, M.E.Doc supply chain), SolarWinds (2020, SUNBURST), Colonial Pipeline (2021, no MFA), Log4Shell (2021, JNDI).
- **Dwell Time:** Average industry dwell time ≈ 194 days. KTU loves this statistic.
- **MTTD vs MTTR:** Mean Time to Detect vs Mean Time to Respond.
- **UTC Timestamps:** Always use UTC for cross-host correlation in super-timelines.
- **Regulatory Disclosure:** GDPR = 72 hours; IT Act India = CERT-In directives 2022 mandate 6-hour reporting for certain incidents.
- **Forensic Tools to Remember:** Wireshark, Volatility, Plaso/log2timeline, Velociraptor, FTK Imager, Autopsy, YARA, TheHive/Cortex.
- **Defense Mapping:** Every kill chain stage must map to a defender control (email gateway, EDR, NDR, SIEM, DLP).
- **Evidence Integrity Formula (Verbal):** Original → Working Copy → Verification Hash → Court Presentation. Never work on the original evidence.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Case Studies: Network Breach Investigations**
> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT755 - CYBER AND NETWORK FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laws and Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Laws and Regulations" in Cyber Forensics?

In the context of **Cyber and Network Forensics**, *Laws and Regulations* refer to the body of statutory provisions, judicial precedents, and international frameworks that govern the **investigation, collection, preservation, and presentation of digital evidence** in a court of law. They define what constitutes a cybercrime, prescribe punishments, specify procedural safeguards, and establish the **legal admissibility** of electronically stored information (ESI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Laws and Regulations in cyber forensics encompass the legal frameworks — national and international — that empower forensic investigators to lawfully acquire digital evidence, define cyber offences, and provide statutory recognition to electronic records.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rulebook of the Digital Detective"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a forensic detective as a **chess player**. The forensic tools and techniques (covered in Module 2) are the *chess pieces*, but the **laws and regulations** are the *official rulebook* of the game. A player cannot arbitrarily capture the king, capture pieces off the board, or peek at the opponent's hand — these are bound by rules. Similarly, a forensic investigator cannot hack into a suspect's computer arbitrarily, seize hard drives without proper authorization, or present chat logs as evidence without a proper chain of custody certificate. The **laws** tell the investigator **what they CAN do, what they CANNOT do, and what happens if they do something wrong**.

### 1.3 Why Are Laws Critical for a Cyber Forensics Investigator?

A technically brilliant forensic analysis is **legally worthless** if it does not satisfy statutory admissibility requirements. The three pillars of forensic legality are:

1. **Authority** — Did the investigator have legal sanction to seize the device? (e.g., warrant under Section 165 CrPC or Section 69 IT Act)
2. **Integrity** — Was the digital evidence preserved without tampering? (Hash matching, Chain of Custody)
3. **Admissibility** — Will a court accept this evidence under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic maps to **Course Outcome (CO3):** *"Comprehend the legal and regulatory framework governing cyber forensics and digital evidence."*

### 1.4 Categories of Laws Covered

| Category | Examples | Jurisdiction |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| **Primary Cyber Law** | Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008) | India |
| **Evidence Law** | Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (Sections 3, 45, 65B) | India |
| **Criminal Procedure** | Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (Sections 91, 165, 173) | India |
| **Data Protection** | Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023 | India |
| **International Conventions** | Budapest Convention (2001) | Council of Europe |
| **Regional/Global Privacy** | GDPR (EU), HIPAA (USA) | EU / USA |
| **Standards** | ISO/IEC 27037, 27042, 27043 | Global |

### 1.5 Key Legal Terminology — Foundational Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> Students must internalize these terms as they appear **repeatedly** in KTU questions and examiner evaluations:

- **Electronic Record (ER):** Any data, record, or information generated, stored, or transmitted in electronic form [IT Act §2(t)].
- **Computer Resource:** Includes computer, computer system, computer network, data, database, or software [IT Act §2(k)].
- **Cyber Forensics:** The application of scientifically proven methods to gather, process, interpret, and use digital evidence in legal proceedings.
- **Admissibility:** The legal quality that makes a piece of evidence acceptable in court.
- **Chain of Custody:** The unbroken, documented chronological trail of seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition of digital evidence.
- **Hearsay:** An out-of-court statement offered to prove the truth of the matter asserted (problematic for digital evidence — overcome via Section 65B).
- **Mens Rea:** Mental intention to commit a crime (essential in many cyber offences).
- **Actus Reus:** The physical act of committing the crime.

### 1.6 The Three-Layer Legal Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Framework — "The Cyber Law Pyramid"**

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────┐
                    │   CONSTITUTIONAL LAYER    │   (Right to Privacy - Puttaswamy 2017)
                    │   (Article 21, 14, 19)    │
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │    STATUTORY LAYER        │   (IT Act 2000, Evidence Act, CrPC)
                    │                           │
                    ├──────────────────────────┤
                    │  PROCEDURAL & STANDARDS   │   (Section 65B certificates, ISO 27037)
                    │       LAYER               │
                    └──────────────────────────┘
```

A forensic investigator must work **upward** through all three layers to ensure any action is constitutionally valid, statutorily authorized, and procedurally compliant.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Information Technology Act, 2000 — The Cornerstone

The **Information Technology Act, 2000** (effective from **17 October 2000**) is India's primary cyber law, enacted based on the **UNCITRAL Model Law on E-Commerce (1996)**. It was **substantially amended in 2008** to address newer forms of cyber crimes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Foundation:** The IT Act 2000 gives **legal recognition to electronic records and digital signatures**, defines **cyber offences**, and establishes the **Cyber Appellate Tribunal (CAT)** and **Adjudicating Officers**.

### 2.2 Key Sections of the IT Act — Forensic Investigator's Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Section Map (Direct KTU Favourite):**

#### 2.2.1 Section 65 — Tampering with Computer Source Documents

- **Offence:** Knowingly concealing, destroying, or altering any computer source code (programs, instructions) when required by law to be maintained.
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or both.
- **Forensic Relevance:** Directly applies when an accused attempts to destroy logs or source code post-incident.

#### 2.2.2 Section 66 — Computer-Related Offences (General)

- **Offence:** Dishonestly or fraudulently doing any act referred to in Section 43 (damage to computer, downloading data, disrupting system).
- **Punishment:** Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or fine up to **₹5 lakh**, or both.
- **Key Difference from §43:** §43 is a *civil wrong*; §66 is a *criminal offence* requiring mens rea.

#### 2.2.3 Section 66A (Struck Down) — Historical Note

- Originally criminalized "offensive messages" online.
- **Declared unconstitutional** by the Supreme Court in *Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)* for violating Article 19(1)(a).

#### 2.2.4 Sections 66B to 66F — Specific Cyber Crimes

| Section | Offence | Punishment |
|---------|---------|------------|
| **66B** | Receiving stolen computer resource or communication device | 3 years / ₹1 lakh |
| **66C** | Identity theft / Fraudulently using digital signature of another | 3 years / ₹1 lakh |
| **66D** | Cheating by personation using communication device/computer | 3 years / ₹1 lakh |
| **66E** | Violation of privacy — capturing, publishing private images | 3 years / ₹2 lakh |
| **66F** | **Cyber Terrorism** — Definitionally the most serious cyber crime | **Imprisonment up to life** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Section 66 (general)** with **66B–66F (specific)**. Examiners test this by giving scenarios — you must identify the **specific section** based on the *actus reus* described.

#### 2.2.5 Sections 67, 67A, 67B — Obscenity and Child Pornography

| Section | Substance | Punishment |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| **67** | Publishing obscene material in electronic form | 3 years + ₹5 lakh (first); 5 years + ₹10 lakh (repeat) |
| **67A** | Publishing sexually explicit material | 5 years + ₹10 lakh (first); 7 years + ₹10 lakh (repeat) |
| **67B** | Publishing child pornography | **7 years + ₹10 lakh** (first); **10 years + ₹10 lakh** (repeat) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Application:** Content moderation systems on social platforms (Meta, X, YouTube) deploy forensic image-hashing algorithms (PhotoDNA, MD5 perceptual hashing) precisely to detect Section 67B content at scale.

#### 2.2.6 Sections 69, 69A, 69B — Interception, Blocking & Monitoring

These are the **most powerful investigation-enabling sections**:

- **Section 69:** Government interception of electronic communication — used by **Intelligence Bureau (IB), RAW, CBI**, with strict safeguards.
- **Section 69A:** Government may direct agencies to **block public access** to information (implemented by **MeitY** through intermediaries under IT Rules 2009/2021). Basis for banning websites, apps, and tweets.
- **Section 69B:** Authorizes **CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team)** to collect traffic data and issue directions for cyber incident response.

#### 2.2.7 Section 79 — Safe Harbour for Intermediaries

- Protects **intermediaries** (ISPs, hosting providers, social media) from liability for third-party content if they act within statutory due-diligence framework.

### 2.3 Indian Evidence Act, 1872 — Digital Evidence Admissibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 65B — THE MOST CRITICAL SECTION FOR FORENSIC INVESTIGATORS**

Section 65B creates a **statutory exception to the hearsay rule** for electronic records. Without a compliant 65B certificate, electronic evidence is **inadmissible**.

#### 2.3.1 Mandatory Requirements of 65B Certificate

A certificate must accompany electronic evidence stating:

1. The **output was produced** during the **ordinary course of business** of a person who is a "lawful occupier" of a computer.
2. Such computer was **operating properly** (or any malfunction does not affect the accuracy of output).
3. The information reproduces or derives from information fed into the computer in the **ordinary course of business**.

#### 2.3.2 Landmark Case Law

> [!NOTE]
> ***Anvar P.V. v. P.K. Basheer (2014)*** & ***Arjun Khotkar v. State of Maharashtra (2020)***:
> The **certificate under Section 65B(4)** is *mandatory* and cannot be replaced by oral evidence. The person producing it must be the **person who "lawfully" possessed the computer** or had **lawful control** over it.

> [!TIP]
> **Prosecution must produce the 65B certificate; failure → evidence expunged.**

### 2.4 Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act, 2023

Passed by Parliament on **9 August 2023**, replacing the older Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019. Key provisions affecting forensics:

| Provision | Forensic Impact |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **§4** Lawful processing | Investigators processing personal data of victims/suspects must have lawful basis |
| **§8** Notice obligation | Data Fiduciaries must inform subjects; affects covert monitoring |
| **§11–17** Rights of Data Principal | Right to erasure may conflict with evidence preservation |
| **§33** Exemptions for State | Allows law enforcement processing without consent under specified conditions |
| **§8(4)** Exemptions for cybersecurity | Data processed for cybersecurity purposes gets exemptions |

### 2.5 Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (CrPC)

Sections most relevant to digital investigation:

- **Section 91** — Summons to produce documents; covers digital documents.
- **Section 165** — Search by police officer; empowers seizure of computers/devices.
- **Section 173** — Reporting of FIR and chargesheet; cyber crime cases registered under appropriate IT Act sections.
- **Section 161** — Examination of witnesses (covers interrogation of accused in cyber cases).

> [!NOTE]
> From the **2024 KTU Scheme**, students should note that the **Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023** has replaced CrPC in stages. However, for the current syllabus context, CrPC remains the operative reference in most board exam questions.

### 2.6 International Frameworks — Comparative Analysis

#### 2.6.1 Budapest Convention on Cybercrime (2001)

The **first international treaty** addressing cybercrime, drafted by the **Council of Europe**. India is a **non-signatory** but uses it as a reference framework.

Key pillars:
- **Substantive Criminal Law:** Harmonization of offences (illegal access, data interference, computer fraud, child pornography).
- **Procedural Law:** Search and seizure of computer data, real-time collection of traffic data, preservation orders.
- **International Cooperation:** 24/7 network for mutual legal assistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks: *"Why has India not signed the Budapest Convention?"* — Answer: Sovereignty concerns over data sharing with foreign governments and lack of consonance with Indian constitutional protections.

#### 2.6.2 GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU — 2018)

| Principle | Forensic Tension |
|-----------|------------------|
| Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency | Investigators must justify data processing |
| Purpose Limitation | Data collected for one purpose cannot be used arbitrarily |
| Data Minimization | Investigators must collect only necessary data |
| Storage Limitation | Conflicts with long-term evidence preservation |

#### 2.6.3 HIPAA (USA, 1996)

- Governs Protected Health Information (PHI).
- **Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§ 164.400–414):** Requires notification within 60 days for breaches affecting >500 individuals.

### 2.7 ISO/IEC Standards for Digital Forensics

| Standard | Focus |
|----------|-------|
| **ISO/IEC 27037:2012** | Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of digital evidence |
| **ISO/IEC 27042:2015** | Guidelines for the analysis and interpretation of digital evidence |
| **ISO/IEC 27043:2015** | Incident investigation principles and processes |
| **ISO/IEC 27050** | e-Discovery (collection from electronically stored information for litigation) |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is what examiners expect you to reproduce in 14-mark answers.**

| Offence / Section | Cognizable? | Bailable? | Maximum Punishment | Court |
|-------------------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|-------|
| §65 Tampering with source code | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹2 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66 Computer-related | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹5 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66B Stolen computer | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹1 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66C Identity theft | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹1 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66D Cheating by personation | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹1 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66E Privacy violation | Yes | Yes | 3 yrs / ₹2 lakh | Magistrate |
| §66F Cyber terrorism | Yes | **No** | **Life imprisonment** | Sessions Court |
| §67 Obscenity | Yes | Yes | 3–5 yrs / ₹5–10 lakh | Magistrate |
| §67A Sexually explicit | Yes | Yes | 5–7 yrs / ₹10 lakh | Sessions |
| §67B Child pornography | Yes | **No** | 7–10 yrs / ₹10 lakh | Sessions |
| §69 Interception | — | — | 7 yrs (for disclosure) | Magistrate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cognizable** = Police can arrest without warrant.
> **Bailable** = Bail as a matter of right (unless §66F / 67B).

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SIH/Interpol-Triggered Investigations:** Multinational cooperation under mutual legal assistance treaties (MLAT) and 24/7 contact networks — directly invokes Section 69B and international cooperation.
- **Corporate CSOCs:** Operate under DPDP Act §8(4) cybersecurity exemption for live network monitoring.
- **Court Presentation:** Forensic reports *must* be accompanied by Section 65B(4) certificate — a procedural necessity routinely overlooked by junior analysts.
- **CERT-In Directions (April 2022):** Mandate 6-hour log retention, synchronization with NTP, and reporting of cyber incidents — operational implications of Section 70B.
