# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AI in Digital Forensics. List any two AI techniques used in forensic evidence analysis. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Digital Forensics refers to the application of machine learning algorithms, deep neural networks, and natural language processing techniques to automate and enhance the discovery, classification, and analysis of digital evidence. It transforms manual, time-intensive forensic workflows into intelligent, scalable, and predictive investigative processes.

**Two AI Techniques (1 Mark):**
1. **Random Forest** — An ensemble supervised learning method used for malware family classification based on PE header and opcode features.
2. **Autoencoders** — Unsupervised deep learning models used for anomaly detection by learning compressed representations of normal network traffic and flagging deviations as potential intrusions.

### Question 2: What is Blockchain Forensics? Mention any two challenges faced during cryptocurrency investigation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Blockchain Forensics is the specialized branch of digital forensics that investigates criminal activities involving distributed ledger technologies. It involves tracing cryptocurrency transactions, de-anonymizing pseudonymous wallet addresses, analyzing smart contract exploits, and reconstructing financial crime timelines across decentralized networks.

**Two Challenges (1 Mark):**
1. **Privacy Coins (Monero, Zcash)** — Use advanced cryptographic techniques (ring signatures, zero-knowledge proofs) that make transaction tracing computationally infeasible.
2. **Mixers and Tumblers** — Services that pool and shuffle funds from multiple users, breaking the deterministic on-chain link between source and destination addresses.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the role of Artificial Intelligence in modern cyber forensic investigations. Discuss with examples how Machine Learning and Deep Learning techniques are applied in malware analysis and network anomaly detection. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Role of AI in Cyber Forensics (3 Marks):**

Modern cyber forensic investigations deal with overwhelming volumes of digital evidence — terabytes of disk images, millions of network packets, and thousands of malware samples daily. Traditional manual analysis cannot scale to this volume, velocity, and variety. AI addresses these challenges by:

- **Automating evidence triage:** ML classifiers rapidly prioritize high-value evidence from large forensic images.
- **Detecting unknown threats:** Unsupervised models identify zero-day malware variants that evade signature-based antivirus engines.
- **Predictive threat intelligence:** Time-series models forecast emerging attack patterns from historical logs.
- **Reducing analyst fatigue:** NLP summarization condenses lengthy log files into actionable incident reports.

**2. Machine Learning in Malware Analysis (2 Marks):**

Supervised ML algorithms like **Random Forest**, **XGBoost**, and **Support Vector Machines** classify malware by extracting static features (PE header fields, opcode n-grams, imported DLLs) and dynamic features (API call sequences, registry modifications). For example, the **EMBER dataset** (Endgame Malware BEnchmark for Research) trains Random Forest models on 2,351 features extracted from 1.1 million PE files, achieving **>98% accuracy** in distinguishing malicious from benign binaries. Feature importance scores from these models also provide interpretable forensic evidence for court proceedings.

**3. Deep Learning in Network Anomaly Detection (2 Marks):**

Deep learning models, particularly **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** networks and **Transformer architectures**, excel at sequential pattern recognition in network traffic. An LSTM autoencoder trained exclusively on normal NetFlow records learns a compressed representation; deviations exceeding a threshold (reconstruction error) are flagged as anomalies. **Autoencoders** and **Variational Autoencoders (VAEs)** are deployed in modern **Network Detection and Response (NDR)** platforms like Darktrace and Vectra AI, enabling detection of novel attack vectors such as **Living-off-the-Land (LotL)** attacks where adversaries use legitimate system tools for malicious purposes.

#### (b) With a neat diagram, describe the architecture of an AI-augmented forensic investigation pipeline. Explain each stage in detail. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Stage 1 Evidence Acquisition] --> F2[Stage 2 Pre Processing]
    F2 --> F3[Stage 3 Feature Extraction]
    F3 --> F4[Stage 4 AI Model Inference]
    F4 --> F5[Stage 5 Decision Triage]
    F5 --> F6[Stage 6 Analyst Verification]
    F6 --> F7[Stage 7 Court Ready Report]
```

**Stage-by-Stage Explanation (4 Marks):**

| Stage | Function | Tools/Techniques |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Evidence Acquisition | Capture forensic image, memory dump, network capture in forensically sound manner | EnCase, FTK, Volatility, tcpdump |
| 2. Pre-Processing | Hash verification (SHA-256), deduplication, noise removal | hashlib, Sleuth Kit, bulk_extractor |
| 3. Feature Extraction | Convert raw artifacts to structured numerical vectors | pefile, YARA rules, NetFlow exporters |
| 4. AI Model Inference | Apply trained ML/DL model to generate predictions + confidence | scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch |
| 5. Decision Triage | Route predictions to priority queues based on confidence scores | SOAR platforms, custom Python scripts |
| 6. Analyst Verification | Human-in-the-loop review with explainable AI (XAI) outputs | LIME, SHAP visualizations |
| 7. Court-Ready Report | Generate legally admissible report with chain of custody | EnCase reports, FTK reports, custom templates |

**Key Principle:** The AI augments but does not replace the analyst. The **chain of custody** must be preserved throughout, and every AI-generated inference must be reproducible and explainable to satisfy evidentiary standards like the **Daubert Test**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) What is Blockchain Forensics? Explain the multi-input heuristic and change address heuristic with examples. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Blockchain Forensics Definition (2 Marks):**

Blockchain Forensics is the investigative discipline that analyzes distributed ledger transactions to trace cryptocurrency movements, identify wallet owners, reconstruct financial crime timelines, and provide court-admissible evidence from on-chain data. It combines cryptographic analysis, graph algorithms, OSINT, and legal processes (subpoenas to KYC-compliant exchanges).

**2. Multi-Input Heuristic (2.5 Marks):**

**Principle:** If a transaction has multiple inputs, all input addresses are assumed to be controlled by the same wallet owner, because signing each input requires the corresponding private key.

**Example:** Transaction T consumes inputs from addresses A, B, and C to pay D and E. Under the heuristic, $\{A, B, C\}$ belong to the **same cluster** $\mathcal{C}_1$. If address A later co-spends with address F in transaction T', then F is also added to cluster $\mathcal{C}_1$ by transitivity.

**Exception:** CoinJoin transactions deliberately combine inputs from different users, requiring heuristic refinement.

**3. Change Address Heuristic (2.5 Marks):**

**Principle:** In Bitcoin, when a user spends only part of their Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO), the wallet generates a new address to receive the "change." This new address is controlled by the sender.

**Example:** A user with 5 BTC in address G wants to pay H 2 BTC. The wallet creates transaction T'' sending 2 BTC to H and 3 BTC back to a newly generated address G'. Under the heuristic, G' is owned by the same entity as G.

**Identifying Change Outputs:** Forensic tools use heuristics like "the output not present in the wallet's address book" or "the output to a fresh address with no prior history."

#### (b) Discuss the challenges of investigating cryptocurrency crimes involving privacy coins, mixers, and cross-chain bridges. Suggest mitigation strategies. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Challenge 1: Privacy Coins (2 Marks):**

Monero uses **ring signatures** (mixing the sender's input with 10+ decoy inputs), **stealth addresses** (one-time recipient addresses), and **RingCT** (hidden transaction amounts). Zcash uses **zk-SNARKs** (zero-knowledge proofs) that mathematically prove transaction validity without revealing sender, receiver, or amount. Together, these make transaction graph analysis infeasible.

**Mitigation:** Investigators rely on **off-chain metadata** (IP addresses at exchange endpoints, timing analysis, mandatory exchange delistings reducing Monero liquidity, and behavioral fingerprinting of wallet software). Researchers at Princeton and MIT have demonstrated partial deanonymization of Monero in pre-2017 transactions, but current protocols remain robust.

**Challenge 2: Mixers/Tumblers (2 Marks):**

Centralized mixers (e.g., Bitcoin Fog, Helix) and decentralized protocols (e.g., Tornado Cash, CoinJoin implementations like Wasabi Wallet) pool funds from many users and redistribute them, breaking the deterministic transaction link. An investigator can no longer prove that Address X sent funds to Address Y.

**Mitigation:** Advanced statistical techniques (cluster intersection, peel-chain analysis with timing correlations), Chainalysis's proprietary peel-chain algorithm, and the **Tornado Cash sanctions case (2022)** which demonstrated that even mixer operators can be identified through server logs and developer identities. Post-mix behavior analysis (where funds eventually land at a KYC exchange) remains the most successful deanonymization vector.

**Challenge 3: Cross-Chain Bridges (2 Marks):**

Bridges (e.g., Ronin, Wormhole, Nomad) lock assets on one chain and mint wrapped equivalents on another. Criminals exploit this by laundering funds across chains faster than investigators can trace. The **Ronin Bridge hack (March 2022, $625M)** highlighted the difficulty of cross-chain asset recovery because the legal jurisdiction of the bridge operator is often ambiguous.

**Mitigation:** Real-time cross-chain monitoring tools (TRM Labs, Chainalysis Cross-Chain Screening), cooperation between blockchain analytics firms, and emerging **Chainabuse** platforms that crowdsource intelligence on fraudulent addresses across multiple chains.

**Synthesis (1 Mark):**

Effective cryptocurrency investigation requires a **multi-layered approach**: combining on-chain graph analysis with off-chain OSINT, legal cooperation with global exchanges, and continuous adaptation as privacy-enhancing technologies evolve. AI/ML models trained on historical laundering patterns increasingly assist investigators in prioritizing leads.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Future Trends Answers:**
>
> 1. **Treating AI as magic:** Examiners penalize answers that claim AI "automatically solves" forensic problems without acknowledging **false positives, adversarial attacks on ML models (e.g., adversarial malware samples), and the need for human-in-the-loop validation**.
>
> 2. **Conflating Bitcoin and Ethereum:** Students frequently state that "Ethereum uses SHA-256 mining" — this is wrong since Ethereum's merge transitioned to **Proof-of-Stake**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for such factual errors.
>
> 3. **Skipping limitations:** Answers that present only the benefits of AI/blockchain forensics without discussing **bias in training data, privacy concerns, jurisdictional challenges, or Chain-of-Custody integrity** are marked down by 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers:** KTU's marking scheme awards **2–3 marks specifically for neat labeled diagrams** in long answers. Always include a flowchart or block diagram.
>
> 5. **Weak handwriting of blockchain addresses:** When writing Bitcoin/Ethereum addresses in answers, even one wrong character demonstrates lack of attention to detail. Use clearly distinguishable characters and double-check before submission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **AI in Forensics** = ML + DL + NLP + Computer Vision applied to digital evidence pipelines.
> - **Supervised vs. Unsupervised:** Use supervised when labeled forensic data exists; use unsupervised for anomaly detection on unknown threats.
> - **Cross-Entropy Loss** is the canonical objective function for forensic classifiers; **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1** are the canonical evaluation metrics.
> - **Blockchain Forensics Triad:** Recognize (artifacts on device) → Record (on-chain state) → Reconstruct (timeline).
> - **Multi-Input Heuristic:** Co-input addresses in the same transaction belong to the same owner (with CoinJoin exception).
> - **Change Address Heuristic:** Fresh output addresses receiving leftover funds belong to the sender.
> - **Bitcoin Address** = Base58Check(0x00 ∥ RIPEMD160(SHA256(PublicKey)) ∥ Checksum).
> - **Smart Contract Forensics** uses disassembly (evmdis), static analysis (Slither, Mythril), and symbolic execution (Manticore).
> - **Top forensic tools:** Chainalysis Reactor, Elliptic Investigator, TRM Labs, Crystal Blockchain.
> - **Key challenges:** Privacy coins (Monero, Zcash), mixers (Tornado Cash), cross-chain bridges, deepfake evidence, adversarial ML.
> - **Court admissibility requires:** Chain of custody preservation, reproducibility, explainability (XAI), and Daubert/Davey standards compliance.
> - **AI does NOT replace the analyst:** It augments and accelerates, but human verification and legal process remain essential.
> - **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers — block diagrams, flowcharts, or transaction graphs earn 2–3 marks.
> - **Mention both advantages and limitations** to demonstrate depth and earn full marks.
